channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: Facing AI anxiety
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
Unless you have chosen to stay away from the news and have silenced all the streams of chatter that occupy your phone and other communication channels, it has been practically impossible to ignore the buzz around artificial intelligence. This reached a crescendo during recent weeks, with India hosting the Global AI Summit in New Delhi – which, from all accounts, was quite a mela. All the big names in the tech industry were there, as were all those who are trying to push back against the hype. The mood was largely celebratory, the air filled with excitement and expectation, and the week peppered with multiple photo-ops and snappy bites from industry leaders. The “New Delhi Declaration on AI” was announced, endorsed by around 88 countries, which pledged to support responsible and inclusive AI systems that would benefit society.

I don’t blame you if your eyes are beginning to glaze over and your mind not-so-slowly turning off, recognizing in these words a message one has heard before, from the early age of computers, then the internet and mobile connectivity, riding on the promise of social media, and now, artificial intelligence. We have been inundated with memes and reels that poke fun at our dependence on these technologies while also reinforcing the sense that the machines will soon open up a wonderful new world. We’re left not really knowing what or how to think. Is life going to be better or is it going to get worse? Who will benefit (apart from the companies themselves) and who will be left out? Is the government moving fast enough to regulate use of AI or, in industry parlance, “put guard rails around it”, or is it going to be a free for all, with the technology racing ahead before we can respond with regulation?

It’s really hard to know how to think about it, and how to prepare ourselves for this new world. A parent recently asked me, “How do we AI-proof our children’s futures?” In other words, how do we take advantage of these shiny new tools, and how do we make sure we are not being taken advantage of?

At the edges of the Global AI Summit were groups raising some of the critical issues that regulators need to be thinking of, arguing that we need strong limitations on how and where AI should be used, asking governments to pay attention to the risks for children and other vulnerable groups, and calling for stronger regulations on data capture. Some groups went so far as to say that there should be spaces and aspects of life where AI is not allowed free rein. But those of us who work with children, and are in the job of building thinking individuals, cannot wait for regulation. Instead, we need to protect these spaces of learning and use them to build critical capacities that will weather an AI-suffused world.

And just how do we do that? Teacher Plus has been grappling with this question in different ways. Our columnist Neerja Singh has consistently been looking for ways that teachers and parents can stay “a step ahead” and in this month’s cover story, Kamakshi Balasubramanian speaks of how we can help children become thoughtful information consumers. We’ll keep looking for ways to challenge (and make use of) new technological incursions into education, and we hope you will share your ideas too.
item.excerpt:encoded: Unless you have chosen to stay away from the news and have silenced all the streams of chatter that occupy your phone and other communication channels, it has been practically impossible to ignore the buzz around artificial intelligence.

item.title: Why literacy learning centres make for smart learning
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Supriya Prakash

Literacy learning centers are more than just classroom add-ons — they are smart, intentional tools that bring structure, equity, and measurable impact into the literacy classroom. Done well, centers create space for autonomy, differentiation, teacher-led focus, and authentic evidence of learning.

If the science of reading has taught us anything, it is this: teaching with heart and effort is not enough. What truly matters is whether instruction is working for children and      whether learning can be measured in authentic, organic ways that inform and refine whole-group instruction. Literacy learning c     enters give us exactly that — a feedback loop that pushes instruction toward equity, challenge, and productive struggle.

At the same time, they align beautifully with the developmental needs of young learners, offering short, sharp bursts of focus — 10 to 15 minutes at a time — which is exactly the right cognitive window for early childhood classrooms.

Autonomy building agency in learners

A literacy center is not a worksheet station. It is a space where children can actively try out strategies they have learned, experiment with tools, and engage in productive struggle without constant teacher intervention.

Instead of stepping in too quickly, the teacher deliberately withholds scaffolding, leaving behind quick reference charts or task cards that nudge children to think independently. Materials are multisensory and concrete, suited to the developmental stage of young learners.

Within this space, children learn not only skills but also the art of task completion — collaborating, dabbling, and learning from peers, all without the teacher hovering over their shoulder. This nurtures confidence, independence, and authentic engagement.

Differentiation: meeting learners where they are

Equity in instruction is no longer optional — it is the expectation. Effective teachers know that not all children need the same task, the same way, at the same time. Literacy centers provide an elegant solution.

By designing centers at multiple levels of complexity, teachers ensure that students have access to tasks at the sound, word, and sentence level. Every child has an entry point: some revisit and practice foundational skills, while others extend themselves into more complex applications.

This form of differentiation is natural and non-stigmatizing. Instead of being singled out for one-on-one intervention (which can feel like “me versus the teacher”), children find themselves in a majority setting where everyone is working at their level. Teachers can      observe      or join small groups briefly, offering just-in-time nudges.

The result is a classroom where every child blooms at their own pace.

Teacher-led work

While autonomy and independence are central to learning centers, certain skills demand direct, careful guidance. Letter formation, phoneme awareness, and sound–symbol correspondence are examples of foundational tasks that, if learned incorrectly, are very hard to unlearn.

Teacher-led centers provide this precision. In these small-group sessions, children receive the teacher’s full attention, but in a way that is time-bound and purposeful. For the teacher, these moments offer more than skill correction — they provide a window      into the child’s thinking process, fueling authentic assessments and meaningful parent-teacher conversations.

Here, scaffolds can be deeply personalized, making learning accessible and tailored, while still keeping the environment dynamic and engaging.

Evidence of learning: data that feeds instruction

Perhaps the most powerful feature of literacy centers is the evidence they generate. Centers are designed with outcomes in mind. Teachers set clear expectations for what children should demonstrate, and the tasks provide authentic data points that directly inform next steps.

Because learning centers typically remain in place for one to two weeks, children have repeated opportunities to practice and consolidate their skills. This “rinse and repeat” approach helps students move from exposure to mastery — building confidence along the way.

The formative data gathered through observation, checklists, and student work is both quick and authentic. It reflects not just whether teaching happened, but whether learning stuck.

Over time, these cycles of evidence allow teachers to adapt instruction “just in time” instead of “just in case,” ensuring teaching is always responsive and relevant.

The bottom line

Phonics, word work, and literacy centers are not just classroom activities — they are powerful engines of equitable, evidence-based teaching. They:

  • Give children autonomy and agency.

  • Support natural differentiation without stigma.

  • Provide targeted, teacher-led intervention where it matters most.

  • Generate authentic data that sharpens whole-group instruction.

When designed thoughtfully, literacy learning centers embody the balance between structure and flexibility, between independence and guidance. They create classrooms where every child can thrive — not someday, but today.


Supriya Prakash is a Literacy Coach with 18 years of experience as a teacher, HOD, and mentor. Certified in IB and TCWRP literacy coaching, she blends academic expertise with a passion. She can be reached at Supriya.prakash2@gmail.com
item.excerpt:encoded:
Surprya Prakash
Literacy learning centers are presented as structured, evidence-based tools that support autonomy, differentiation, and targeted teacher instruction. Supriya Prakash explains how these centers can help to generate useful data that can align with the developmental needs of early learners.

item.title: Nurturing hearts, building humane values
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Vikram Thakur

At The Aarambh School, education is not confined to textbooks or classrooms — it is about shaping compassionate hearts and empathetic minds. Every year, the school celebrates Daan Utsav – the Festival of Giving, to help children experience the joy of sharing and imbibe values such as gratitude, empathy, and respect.

As part of this initiative, our students visit various NGOs and institutions — old age homes, orphanages, schools for children with special needs, homes for the visually impaired, government hospitals and animal shelters. Each visit is a journey of awareness and transformation, helping children see life beyond their own world.

One such visit was to an ashram for mentally challenged children. A student shared, “A child there kept looking at my watch with such excitement! I never even think about my watch, but it meant so much to him.” That simple observation taught him the true meaning of gratitude — to value what we often take for granted. Another student reflected, “They were so joyful! They didn’t envy anyone and were happy all the time.” Such reflections show how children begin to understand that happiness lies in contentment, not in possessions.

The experience led to an even more touching act. One student, deeply moved, said, “Can we all come together and buy watches for them?” The group immediately contributed, and within two days, they were ready with the gifts. When they returned to the ashram and handed out the gifts, the joy on everyone’s faces was indescribable — a moment that captured the pure essence of giving.

Another heartwarming experience happened during a visit to an old age home. The children interacted, danced, and shared stories with the elderly residents. The happiness on the seniors’ faces was unmistakable — a reflection of how deeply they missed their own grandchildren. After returning, a group of students approached me and said, “Sir, can you please help us go back to meet them? Or maybe we can get the phone numbers of their children? We want to tell them that no one should ever leave their parents” Hearing such words from 10- and 11-year-olds brought tears to my eyes. In that moment, I knew — we are truly nurturing a better society.

Our students also visited an animal shelter, where they saw injured and paralyzed dogs — some victims of accidents, others harmed intentionally. Witnessing their suffering moved the children deeply; many of them cried. Since then, their attitude toward animals has changed — they no longer throw stones or act carelessly, but instead show sensitivity and kindness toward every living being.

Another memorable visit was to the railway station, where students served food to those in need. One man came up to me and said, “I came to this city today to appear for a competitive exam. I somehow missed my last train and didn’t have money to stay back or buy food. I was praying to God — and then I saw the children serving meals. I felt that God truly listens!” I shared this incident with my students  and his words made them realize how even the smallest acts of kindness can make a world of difference.

During Daan Utsav, our celebrations also include acts of appreciation for our support staff. This year, children brought food from home and served it to them, expressing their gratitude for the hard work and care they receive every day. The smiles, laughter, and sense of togetherness made it a truly special moment for everyone.

Through such experiences, Daan Utsav at The Aarambh School has become more than a celebration — it is a journey of emotional growth. It teaches our children that giving is not just about material things; it is about sharing joy, spreading kindness, and building connections that touch the heart.

These moments remind us, as educators, that education is truly complete when it awakens the spirit of humanity.

As it is said, “The deeds of giving are the very foundations of the world.”

And at the end of the day, it’s not about what you have or even what you have accomplished — it’s about who you have lifted up, who you have made better, and what you have given back.

Vikram Thakur is the co-founder of The Aarambh School. He believes that education is the most powerful tool to bring positive change in society. Through The Aarambh School, his vision is to nurture a generation that is not only knowledgeable but also empathetic, compassionate, and responsible — individuals who contribute meaningfully to building a better world. He can be reached at vikram@aarambhschool.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Vikran Thakur
At The Aarambh School, children experience, imbibe and practice values of empathy and gratitude. Visits to ashrams, old age homes, hospitals, and animal shelters, reshape how they see happiness, kindness, and responsibility.

item.title: Building psychological strength of adolescent Indians
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Pradeep Waychal

Indian adolescents are navigating a complex landscape of academic pressure, rapid digitalization, high family expectations, and career uncertainties. One in seven Indian adolescents experiences mental health challenges, resulting in increased instances of depression, anxiety and stress      (Source– A 2024 National Mental health Survey). Developing psychological strength can help manage stressful thoughts, regulate emotions, and improve productivity.

Psychological strength is a complex and multi-dimensional concept.      We have defined it to consist of Gratitude, Resilience, Achievement-orientation, Positivity, Emotional intelligence, and a Sophophilic (knowledge-oriented) mindset – collectively called GRAPES Strengthening GRAPES      can ensure adolescents not only survive, but also thrive, leading to a healthy and progressive society and nation.

Gratitude

Gratitude is a thankful appreciation for what an individual receives, both tangible and intangible. It shifts focus from scarcity to abundance, enhancing contentment and motivation. Gratitude also helps people connect to something larger than themselves – to other people, nature, or a higher power, and initiates a virtuous cycle. Individuals practicing daily gratitude journaling reported a 23% reduction in stress levels and a 17% improvement in sleep quality (Source- 2024- study by Indian Council of Medical Research).

Resilience

Resilience is the ability to withstand adversity and bounce back from failures. This is especially critical in India, where limited opportunities often lead to a higher rate of failures. Resilient individuals also experience stress and emotional upheaval but they manage and control such situations with focus, perseverance and grit. The resilience is not innate and can be systematically built through self-reflection and learning from setbacks, along with mentoring.

Achievement orientation

The progress of humanity is primed by the passion to achieve something significant in life - to set lofty goals and accomplish them. Achievement orientation aligns efforts with purpose. It goes beyond mere academic grades. It includes taking calculated risks and persisting through challenges. It helps channelize energy towards one’s goals. It requires embracing deep learning and seeing setbacks as opportunities for growth, rather than defining one’s worth.   

Positivity

Positivity is the ability to identify opportunities within challenges, while acknowledging reality. It is the practice of focusing on the good in any situation and looking at setbacks as temporary. Positivity enables adolescents to reframe challenges, support creative problem-solving and have a positive impact on their physical and mental health. Individuals who engaged in positive self-talk and reframed negative experiences showed a 28% improvement in their perceived quality of life (Happiness Survey India (2024)).

Emotional intelligence

Emotional Intelligence (EI) involves recognizing, understanding, and managing emotions in oneself and others in positive ways to relieve stress, communicate effectively, empathize with others, and defuse conflict. It helps in handling interpersonal challenges and nurturing meaningful relationships, reducing isolation and increasing support systems during adversity. A 2023 NASSCOM Talent Report revealed that leaders with high EI outperformed their peers by 20% in managing team conflicts and building trust.

Sophophilic mindset

A Sophophilic mindset -- or love for learning -- goes beyond information gathering into developing wisdom. It expands perspectives and increases adaptability. It fosters curiosity, humility, and open-mindedness. An All India Survey on Higher Education report (2024) noted that students engaged in voluntary learning projects outside their curriculum, demonstrated 25% higher problem-solving skills and adaptability - the critical 21st century skills.

How can parents and others develop a GRAPES mindset

The simplest way is they themselves live as per the above six dimensions. They can present and discuss case-studies of individuals who have displayed these strengths. Developing GRAPES needs equal importance and attention as academics. Promote frank conversations with adolescents so that they can freely discuss their feelings and challenges without fear of judgment. Inculcate the habit of ‘journaling’ in adolescents.

For gratitude, adolescents can regularly share things for which they are grateful with their near and dear ones. For resilience, adolescents must attempt solving problems by themselves to the extent possible or with support form parents. They must learn to accept failures and parents must assure them that failures don’t define their worth. For achievement orientation, adolescents can be guided to set their missions and micro-goals aligned with it. They should be praised for their methods, not just the results. Positivity can be inculcated by reframing challenges through self-evaluation and looking at the silver lining. Emotional intelligence can be developed via reflective exercises and active debates. A Sophophilic mindset can be cultivated by developing lifelong learning habits. This includes promoting reading and structured learning of new topics through real-life projects.

Conclusion    

In a rapidly evolving India, psychological strength will be a bridge between potential and realized achievements. By nurturing a GRAPES mindset, we can      build a society and country that is both materially and spiritually successful.

Dr. Pradeep Waychal is a Managing Trustee at Guruji Education Foundation. He can be reached at pradeep.waychal@gurujifoundation.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Pradeep Waychal
With rising academic pressure, digital exposure, and career uncertainty, Indian adolescents face growing mental health challenges. This article introduces the GRAPES framework as a structured approach to building psychological strength.

item.title: What changes can be made in education?
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Sandipkumar Natvarbhai Prajapati

Education is an essential pillar of society. A country whose education is competent can reach new heights of development. In today's modern era, when technology and scientific discoveries are constantly advancing, there is a need to improve our educational sector according to the times. For the new generation, there should be such an education that is not limited to just book knowledge, but teaches them how to face the complex situations of life.

Practice-based education    

 Beyond books, children should be taught things that are useful in life. Such as — banking, time management, health, and first aid. Currently, Bagless Day is being celebrated in schools. As part of this celebration, we can give children  practical knowledge in these areas. We can make children visit banks, hospitals, and visit various businesses and industries. We can assign children various activities so that they learn time management. Role play can also be taught on how to help others in times of disaster.

Technology-based education    

 Education can be made more interesting and easy through digital platforms, virtual classrooms and AI-based tools. Students should be encouraged to do projects and multimedia presentations. For this, smart classrooms have been built in almost all schools in Gujarat. Online  quizzes can help them self-evaluate and see what mistakes they have made. Children can also be taught coding through applications like Scratch and Picto Block. Through this, children gain knowledge of technology as their decision-making power and intelligence develop.      

Interdisciplinary approach    

  When multiple subjects are taught in a single activity or project, it is called an interdisciplinary approach where we can combine lie skills and technology with subjects like mathematics, science, history and language. For example, data analysis in mathematics, digital presentation in history, and so on. With this approach, children can learn better by combining different subjects and gain direct experience from life. Here’s an example:    

Example : Water saving campaign

Subjects : Science + Mathematics + Language + Art

Activity : Teaching ways to save water.

  1. Make them calculate how much water is used in their daily routine.
  • Write a formula on the topic of ‘Save Water’.
  • Make a poster.

This activity connects the following subjects:    

Science: by highlighting the importance of water

Mathematics: through water consumption accounting

Language: formula/text

Art: Poster

Balance of knowledge and skills    

 Professional skills should also be developed in the student, wherein IT, digital marketing, graphic design, multimedia, handicrafts etc. should be taught. The child should have both the ability to know and do. Knowledge means getting information, understanding things, knowing (or being able to find) the answers to questions, and learning rules and principles. For example, knowledge implies knowing that water boils at a temperature of 100 degrees Celsius. However, skill means working with what you know, and using the experience of study in life. If there is only knowledge and no skills, a person would know a lot but have no ability to apply their knowledge. If there is only skill and no knowledge, a person can do the work, but would not have the ability to understand the reason for it. Therefore, the balance of both is very important in the all-round development of a      child. “It is necessary to know and also to show!” Skill without knowledge is incomplete and knowledge without skill is like the pages of an empty book. Together, both create true all-round development.

Life values ​​and moral education    

 Moral education is also important for instilling humanism, patriotism, understanding of right and wrong and inspiration for living in students. The good principles, ways of doing things and humane values ​​in our life, which we adopt in life, are called life values. For example, speaking the truth, being kind, cooperating, working hard, being honest, etc. All these are the main foundations of knowledge, behaviour and personality. The approach to teaching children good qualities, culture and morality is moral education. The education that makes them understand the difference between right and wrong, makes them good citizens, teaches them discipline, humanity, and responsibility is called moral education.      

Innovative training for teachers    

 It is also necessary to train teachers in new technology and teaching methods, so that they can prepare students as per the needs and requirements of the rapidly changing modern world.    

Conclusion         

In summary, it can be said that education is not only a tool for imparting knowledge, but also for overall personal and social development. If timely changes are not brought, the education system will become traditional and the future generation will be left behind. Therefore, the new era requires an education system that develops technology, life values, practical skills and the ability to face the problems of life.

Sandipkumar Natvarbhai Prajapati is a teacher at the Primary School in Gujarat’s Kheda district. He can be reached at prajapatisandy1989@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sandipkumar Natvarbhai Prajapati
Moving beyond textbook learning, this piece highlights how interdisciplinary learning can make education more relevant and engaging for today’s students.

item.title: Silent struggles: the mental health crisis among private school teachers
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Rajvir Singh

In a time when education is evolving rapidly, the mental health of those imparting knowledge—our teachers—is increasingly under threat. Once considered a stable and fulfilling profession, teaching, especially in private schools, has become a high-pressure occupation riddled with stress, exhaustion, and emotional burnout.

Today’s private school teacher wears many hats: instructor, event organizer, data handler, and sometimes even counsellor. The workload has ballooned far beyond classroom instruction. Teachers not only conduct back-to-back lessons but also carry home heaps of notebooks for correction, prepare lesson plans, manage student behaviour, and plan extra-curricular events. The concept of “free time” is a distant dream.

"Teaching used to be a noble and relaxed profession. Now, I feel like I’m constantly racing against the clock," says a private school teacher who wished to remain anonymous.

Adding to this stress is the increasing interference from parents. Teachers often face unrealistic expectations and frequent complaints—ranging from student performance to teaching styles. The pressure to please parents can lead to manipulation of grades or ignoring disciplinary issues, further impacting the teacher’s sense of integrity and control in the classroom.

"Sometimes, I spend more time justifying my methods to parents than actually planning lessons for my students," one educator shared.

What’s more, many teachers feel stifled by a lack of autonomy. Despite their training and experience, school managements often impose rigid policies, dictate curriculum content, and demand frequent documentation. The trust and respect once naturally extended to educators have, in many cases, been replaced with surveillance and scepticism.

Technology, too, has contributed to the problem. While it has improved accessibility and connectivity, it has also blurred the boundaries between personal and professional life. Teachers are now expected to be available after school hours on messaging apps and online platforms, responding to queries and assigning or evaluating homework digitally.

All these factors contribute to a steep decline in teachers' mental well-being. Issues such as chronic fatigue, anxiety, depression, and burnout are alarmingly common but remain largely unaddressed due to lack of institutional support or mental health resources within schools.

What’s the way forward?

Experts suggest that the solution lies in structural reforms. Redistributing workloads, setting boundaries on parent-teacher interactions, conducting mental health awareness programmes, and offering professional counselling are steps in the right direction. School administrations must also create channels for open communication and feedback, where teachers can voice concerns without fear of judgment or reprisal.

If we truly value education, we must start valuing      educators. Supporting their mental health is not just an act of compassion—it’s an investment in the future of our children.

Rajvir Singh is a mathematics teachers at Paramount Public School, Lehra District, Sangrur, Punjab. He can be reached at Me.rajvir61@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Rajvir Singh
From back-to-back lessons and endless corrections to after-hours digital availability, private school teachers face an ever-expanding workload. This piece explores how these pressures are affecting teachers’ mental health and what structural reforms may be needed.

item.title: Beyond likes and hashtags: cultivating a critical attitude
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Kamakshi Balasubramanian

The influence of social media on young minds, particularly Gen Z, has lately come under the scrutiny of educators and parents around the world. Consider this: Mark Zuckerberg of Meta and CEOs of three other social media companies* have been dragged to court by more than 1500 parents and school districts in the United States. They have to defend themselves against the allegation that their companies intentionally spread information that affects the mental health and safety of young people. Court hearings have begun. Two of these companies have already entered into out-of-court-settlements with the plaintiffs.

How and why did this happen in a world where life without social media is nearly inconceivable? What is it about our relationship with information that has made it possible for these companies to gain such influence over what and how we consume and share, and, more importantly, what it does to our thinking? This article attempts to first unpack this relationship and then offer some ways in which we can build a more critical attitude towards the (mis)information deluge from social media.

Social media’s unique position

We enjoy discovering new facts. Our brains crave them.

What’s wrong in seeking information? Nothing at all. Thinking skills are a vital part of our mental tool kit. It includes memory, attention, and alert reasoning. Without new facts, these skills will get dull. So why then are we criticizing social media, the source of much information?

It would appear that Gen Z are extremely susceptible to feeds on social media. They rely almost exclusively on social media for information of every kind, whether it is daily news, study tips, cooking lessons, gossip, sports, or a myriad other things about which the human mind is curious. Digital natives not only consume information, but also avidly create content.

Social media platforms have become the primary source of information for many because of the speed of access and attractive packaging. Besides, packets of information on social media tend to be brief. A ping on your device alerts you that there’s a news flash or a new post on your favourite platform. It’s hard to resist that ping, which is somehow always tantalizingly worded.

Why the rush to form an opinion (and to shout it from the rooftops?)

Digital technology is a tool, yes, but why do we have an impulsive need to share what we know and think?

People studying this phenomenon have attempted to isolate the unique characteristics of digital natives, the most important being that they were born and raised in a world in which smart phones and tablets were everywhere. Having grown up with the technology, Gen Z feel a connection and ease with its “culture” of a virtual world and instant communication. Researchers talk about Gen Z’s need to stay connected with their subculture or their community. Humans tend to trade questioning and critical thinking for untested opinions as facts or even truths, and this gets magnified in today’s digital world. The irresponsible, reckless, and sometimes dangerous reliance on social networks for information raises no red flags for many young people as that is their world. Responses to posts are almost instant on social media, which makes the consumer mimic that ability and respond equally quickly. If one takes time, the response loses its impact and receives no “Likes.” That need for recognition in the social media community interferes with the value of analyzing the veracity of a feed. Studies point out how teachers and mentors of today speak a language that predates the language of Gen Z. The gap between digital natives and the earlier generation is wider than it ever was between any other generations. This is a major reason for teachers struggling to guide students.

The fragmented world of social interaction

Interaction with social media feeds is more or less a private matter, without much concurrent input from others who might have their own perspectives on an issue. Among others, the late Rob Reiner once commented that, now, even entertainment is an individual pursuit – whereas in the earlier days, when TV shows were limited to the time of their telecast and movies could be seen only in theatres, families and friends watched them together and informally discussed them. That interaction gets minimized today. Imagine a young person who receives feeds based on his previous clicks, who consumes information chosen by algorithms, sometimes created by AI, nearly always with no citation of a source, and has no verbal interaction with other humans to process the feed. (Interaction does occur, mostly sequentially, but through emojis, and that’s a language at which Gen Z excel). If at all there are other humans (say in chat rooms), with whom Gen Z do interact, they are likely to belong to the same peer group with shared interests and biases.

The social media lure

So far we have spoken about social media’s unique ways of influencing the consumer: the impact of speed and reach, attractive packaging, the consumer’s minimal expectation for factual accuracy in return for “knowing things first,” and the algorithms that determine what you are going to see based on your clicks. Now to all of this, add AI's engaging storytelling formats, intriguing and amusing visuals, and the sheer repetition of a single news item. These are only a few of the ways in which we are bombarded with information we scarcely have the time to absorb and investigate. Before we have registered one headline, another sensational feed stops us in our tracks.

Feeds that can mislead

What kinds of feeds am I talking about? We know the popular medical feeds that make the rounds repeatedly. Have you read the one that says ginger is many times more effective in killing cancer cells than chemotherapy?

Ideological lies spread like wildfire. For example, condemning all communists as anarchists is not correct.

Cultural lies target whole groups. Branding an entire race as inferior or terrorists or calling a certain demographic filthy for their food choices breeds fear and hate.

Let us take educational misinformation. Some people believe that college life or hostel life is not good for women, because that will give women “too much” freedom. This results in women losing their chance to earn, lead, and obviously learn independence.

There are whole groups of people who think that gadgets can replace teachers or that online courses can beat real classrooms. We saw the reality or that falsehood during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Combating misinformation

Why should we take the information flooding the social media and its influence on opinion-making with caution? Because a good deal of the information we see on social media is baseless and unfounded without evidence or facts to support it.

Our responsibility as mentors, parents, and teachers is to be aware of the gaps that misinformation creates in young minds, given how attractively it is packaged and how easy it is to access and spread. There is a large body of research on the addictive potential of social media, and like all serious addictions, social media can and is claimed to have affected not only intellectual skills but mental wellness in the gullible.

In what follows, I shall primarily consider how a teacher might usefully demonstrate the need to approach social media feeds with discrimination. Some of these suggestions are relevant in the home as well, for mentors and parents.

In sum, it is essential to get our students to understand that social media feeds need to be questioned for factual credibility, to check if the source of a feed is reliable, and what opinion-making biases are hiding in the feed. Students need to be encouraged, if not formally trained, to be discerning and to separate the grain from the chaff, not only to evolve as thinkers but also to be safe from misinformation that can endanger their wellbeing.

In the classroom

Encouraging vocal and written expression of ideas and opinions      

First and foremost, it's important for teachers to keep in mind that most of our students are at an age where it is better to encourage them to express an opinion, both written and oral, whether or not well-founded. I know this sounds paradoxical, but I want to make the point that it is better to let a young person express an opinion than to shut them up with criticism. Especially in the presence of adults who can guide them towards discernment, speaking up has to be encouraged.

At this vulnerable stage in a young person’s life, it is important for teachers, mentors, and parents to listen to opinions, even the shaky and untenable ones. Sharp words from adults that draw attention to flaws in arguments kill their urge to speak. Pointed questions often silence young people in oral discourse.

Expression of opinions is important, because it is through expression that people find their voices. Passionate outbursts actually build strength, particularly when followed by the opportunity to reflect on what has been said. Youngsters learn who they are when they speak boldly and when teachers use that as a teachable moment to introduce discernment, critical inquiry, and open mindedness.

Research supports this claim, as studies have shown that young people are less likely to speak up or express themselves in writing, if they are corrected frequently. Instead of correcting the young speaker at every turn, the teacher or the mentor has many other options. Some of these options are simple and widely practised; a couple of which, I discuss below.

Active Listening

It is important that you as a teacher engage in active listening, a skill which eventually you should try and introduce to your class. What does active listening in this situation mean?

A student claims that economical use of water in small apartments or planting trees in open spaces of the school don’t really help when jet engines continue to guzzle fuel and rivers ceaselessly carry polluted waters from industrial waste. You could reword your student’s thought and then ask, “In such a situation, whose responsibility is it to address environmental degradation?” This encourages a dialogue because it acknowledges the speaker’s thought on a subject without any adverse comment. The question is open-ended, which requires more than a yes or no response.

On the contrary, a challenging question, such as, “How did you come to that conclusion? Where is your evidence?” could easily turn into a debate where the speaker might feel the need to defend their position, come out on top, and win a point.

After this approach through paraphrasing, you could ask the entire group a follow-up question. “Now, how many of you feel that this is the case?” or “Is there anybody here who disagrees with that viewpoint?” That is enough to get the group thinking. It is very likely that someone will comment on why they disagree with that viewpoint. This is a good time to ask the entire group if anyone has data or statistics to support either perspective.

When we give the youthful speaker the chance to revisit their opinion, testing it for clarity or even for its reliability, critical engagement with ideas comes more naturally. Written work is different, although a sensitive educator will make comments that are constructive and not fault-finding. Rather than write, “Evidence?”, the teacher could write “Reliable data would support your point.” This will gradually shape one’s future approach to information that comes disguised as opinion, or, worse still, verified knowledge.

Fostering dialogue

Let’s probe opinions with care. The objective is to guide the student to look inward. Introspection builds strong thinking. It checks biases in views.

A teacher’s follow-up questions ought to motivate the student to dig for the sources that shaped an opinion. Try this: “Do you have a gut-feeling about this issue? Or did a book suggest it? What about the social media feeds you have seen? Did your own experience lead you to it?”

If a book is mentioned, express authentic curiosity and ask whether the student knows the author’s ideological or political affiliation.

At this point, you could turn to the whole class and ask, “Do you know of any author who disagrees with this viewpoint? Have you read any article that disagrees with this viewpoint?” Your job is to show how different viewpoints exist on the same issue. It is not for you to declare which is correct and which is wrong, or which is more reliable and which is not. Your job is to point out that it is important to acknowledge the existence of, and see different viewpoints.

Open-mindedness

After all, what is being open-minded? An open-minded person is willing, if not to accept, to at least accommodate or acknowledge varying, even opposing perspectives on a subject. So, as a teacher or a mentor, you move the discussion forward. It is up to you to consistently emphasize that differing viewpoints are possible. You need to offer suggestions on how one determines that a source is reliable. You might say that a certain author has been reviewed in many places, whereas so and so author has received very little recognition among peers and colleagues, and leave it at that. Your responsibility is merely to show that to be considered an authority one must have a wide and varied reach, that one must appeal not to just one narrow group. Such general pointers are sufficient for most young people to understand the drift of where you are going. It would be useful to use this style of open-ended questioning to develop discernment.

Using games to encourage digital literacy

Any number of games are available on the internet to teach digital literacy to children from the earliest stage of formal education. Some of these are based on familiar games such as ‘Two Truths and a Lie’ and ‘Identify the Odd One Out’. Most of these games, currently available on the internet, contain allusions to popular US culture, and for that reason, in our classrooms we would be well-advised to adapt them suitably.

Conclusion

As teachers, mentors and parents, we have inherited a historical tradition as educators. Socrates spoke of “true opinion” – an opinion that could seem totally valid, but one arrived at by chance, without examination, without the person having worked through the necessary steps of understanding wherefrom the opinion arose. This is an important point. Simply mouthing opinions, however valid, doesn’t contribute to depth of understanding and learning. Confucius spoke about “Opinion without learning and critical inquiry” as being shallow, and that a shallow opinion will lead to shallow thinking.

The complex issue of opinions without foundation is not new. In our world today, we are dealing with social media, which adds to the difficulty of addressing this phenomenon. We, as teachers and adults, are responsible for mentoring youngsters to be vigilant in responding to the enormous amounts of information that we have at our fingertips. Misinformation and falsehood disseminated in the guise of opinion or knowledge hinder value-based education.

The classroom is one important environment where combating misinformation in its many forms is possible and necessary. The objective of this article has been to foreground the idea that developing open-mindedness and encouraging introspection in our youth is best done through dialogue – with parents, with mentors, and with peers. A teacher in the classroom is a listener, who welcomes expression of ideas that are examined not through confrontation, not through debate, but through teamwork, as learning is increasingly a cooperative endeavour. Developing the ability to stop, reflect, and carefully consider information – such as on social media – is an important way to counter the onslaught of misinformation that young people are faced with.

*TikTok, YouTube, and Snapchat

Kamakshi Balasubramanian is an educator, a life-long learner, and an occasional writer. She can be reached at papukamakshi@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Kamakshi Balasubramanian
Young people today are consumed by reels, shorts, influencers, and sensational headlines. This is a world where information, assimilation, and reaction happen in double quick time, leaving little room for ambiguity, curiosity, and revision. No wonder we find young people today highly opiniated, unyielding, and unwilling to consider alternative viewpoints. It is important that we train young people to identify misinformation and sensationalism. Teacher Plus brings you classroom strategies that will help your students slow down, question sources, and seek the truth.

item.title: “Miss, who taught the first teacher?”
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Priyanka Soman

Tiffin Box Musings is a collection of reflections that arrive without a timetable, a lesson plan, or a clearly labelled theme – much like a teacher’s thoughts during a busy school day. These pieces are drawn from classrooms, corridors, memories, questions, and those small moments that stay behind long after the bell rings. They do not promise conclusions, only curiosity. The one thing they have in common is learning – sometimes planned, often unexpected, and frequently delivered by students when the teacher least expects it. These musings are simply the everyday thoughts of a teacher who is still learning, one tiffin break at a time.

Have you ever been caught off guard by a child’s question? You’re midway through a perfectly structured lesson, feeling rather pleased that everyone seems attentive, when a small hand goes up:

“Miss, who taught the first teacher?”

Suddenly, you’re silenced. Your PowerPoint slide on the water cycle fades into irrelevance. The room shifts. And for a moment, teacher and students are simply fellow travellers in the strange and wonderful business of life.

As J. Krishnamurti once said, “The whole of life, from the moment you are born to the moment you die, is a process of learning.” Not memorizing, not passing exams – but learning.

Cleverness or intelligence?

Let me ask you: what do you think education is? Passing exams? Moving up a grade? Collecting certificates?

We often confuse these things. But cleverness is not intelligence. Cleverness is knowing that the capital of France is Paris. Intelligence is realizing that if you forget your packed lunch on a school trip to Paris, no amount of geography will save you.

“Intelligence is the capacity to perceive the essential, the what is; and to awaken this capacity, in oneself and in others, is education.”

Think back to your own schooling. How much of what you memorized for exams do you actually use now? Be honest. Did Pythagoras’ theorem rescue you when your washing machine broke down? Probably not.

The left brain, the right brain… and lunch

Our system loves the left side of the brain: logic, numbers, reading, writing. Important, yes. But what about the right side: creativity, rhythm, intuition?

I once had a student who turned an entire maths problem into a rap. Did he get full marks? No. Did the rest of the class finally understand fractions? Absolutely.

Another child asked:

“Miss, if the left brain controls the right hand and the right brain controls the left hand, who controls lunch?”

I laughed. But isn’t there wisdom in that? Life isn’t just logic; it’s also laughter, music, movement, and mystery.

“Education is not the cultivation of memory, but the cultivation of intelligence which is the capacity to think freely and without fear.”

Perhaps children understand this far better than adults do.

Lessons beyond the textbook

Think of a time you learnt something unforgettable outside the classroom. For me, it was a school hike. The children slipped, stumbled, and giggled their way up a muddy hill. One girl reached out and said to me, “Don’t worry Miss, you can use my water bottle when you faint.” Mortifying, but strangely moving.

The hike was steep, muddy, and full of slips, but it was there that true learning happened. Children helped each other, shared snacks, and told stories of home. Bonds were formed not from an essay on “teamwork”, but from the lived experience of needing one another.

“The function of education is to create human beings who are integrated and therefore intelligent.”

And I have seen this integration – mind, heart, and body – most clearly in such lived moments.

What about you? Do you remember a school memory that taught you more than a textbook ever did?

The questions that linger

Children have an incredible way of asking questions that stop us in our tracks:

  • “Miss, if exams test what we remember, why don’t you ask us what we had for lunch last week?”
  • “Miss, if time is money, why doesn’t the bank give us more holidays?”
  • “Miss, what if the Earth is just someone else’s marble?”

Can you feel it? That pause, that moment of silence. These are not questions with tidy answers. They are invitations to wonder. And perhaps that is where real education begins.

“It is no measure of health to be well adjusted to a profoundly sick society.”

Perhaps children’s questions remind us not to adjust too comfortably to a broken education system, but to dream of something new.

Where do we go from here?

It would be easier to keep things mechanical. Teach, test, tick the boxes. But easier is not better. Children are not cupboards to be filled or factory products to be standardized. They are individuals – each with their own rhythms, curiosities, and gifts.

So, here’s the question I leave with you: what would education look like if we stopped asking children to be clever, and instead encouraged them to be intelligent, creative, compassionate human beings?

“The whole purpose of education is to awaken intelligence –and intelligence is the capacity to perceive the essential, the true, the what is.”

Because if we don’t, we risk raising children who can recite facts by heart but cannot recognize the wonder in their own hearts. And between you and me, that would be far sadder than any forgotten boiled egg at the bottom of a lunch bag.

References

  • Krishnamurti on Education 2003
  • Education and the Significance of Life was first published in 1953

Note: All quotes mentioned in the article are by J. Krishnamurti.

Priyanka Soman is an educator and writer with a keen interest in integrating the arts, sciences, and storytelling to create meaningful learning experiences. She can be reached at cspriyankaa@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Priyanka Soman
In “Tiffin Box Musings,” the author gently reminds us that the most meaningful learning often slips in through unscripted moments – curious questions, shared laughter, and lived experiences. Drawing on J. Krishnamurti, she contrasts exam-driven “cleverness” with true intelligence: the freedom to perceive, feel, and think deeply without fear. Through stories of creative expression and collective vulnerability, the article invites educators to honour children’s natural wonder and move beyond factory-style schooling, nurturing minds – and hearts – awake to curiosity, compassion, and possibility.

item.title: The bench we forgot to question: Rethinking intimacy and infrastructure in Indian classrooms
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Vivek Prakasam

In 2019, during a long drive from Kochi to Thiruvananthapuram in Kerala, I found myself deep in conversation with a senior official from the higher education department. What began as a routine journey unfolded into a discussion that has remained with me ever since. At the heart of it was a disturbing case that had recently made national headlines: a 12-year-old girl who became pregnant, allegedly by an 11-year-old boy.

Although the incident did not occur within a school premises, it prompted a difficult but essential reflection on the kind of spaces we provide for our children to learn and grow.

Somewhere between the rolling highways of Kerala and the occasional burst of traffic, our conversation turned towards psychology. We spoke about intimate space, a well-studied concept in human behaviour that remains largely missing from educational discourse in India.

Understanding intimate space in the Indian classroom

In psychological terms, intimate space refers to the personal zone extending up to 18 inches from a person’s body. This zone is typically shared only with deeply trusted people such as family members or close friends. Any uninvited transgression into this space, particularly when prolonged or repetitive, can lead to stress, discomfort, or fear (Hall, E. T., 1966. The Hidden Dimension).

And yet, millions of Indian children spend six to eight hours a day, five days a week, seated on shared wooden benches alongside peers they may barely know. These benches, chosen more for durability and budget constraints than comfort, may seem cost-effective on paper but compromise psychological wellbeing in practice. A typical five-foot-long wooden bench in many colleges is meant for two adult students, but often accommodates three or even four, resulting in an uncomfortably cramped arrangement.

A historical shift in learning spaces

Long before wooden benches became the staple feature of Indian classrooms, learning spaces looked very different. It began with the traditional gurukul, where students sat cross-legged under trees or in shaded courtyards on a wooden paata, a flat plank used for seating, absorbing not just knowledge but values and discipline. In madrasas, students gathered on mats in semi-circles around their ustads or maulvis. Even without formal furniture, respect for personal space was deeply embedded in the educational system and its infrastructure.

This changed during the British rule. The colonial education model, especially after Macaulay’s Minute of 1835, borrowed heavily from European church schools. In these institutions, long rows of wooden benches and desks faced a lectern or pulpit, reinforcing a model of hierarchy, discipline, and moral instruction. These design choices, rooted in Christian monastic and ecclesiastical traditions, were adapted in colonial schools as instruments of order and control. In India, this architectural philosophy replaced earlier fluid and interactive forms of learning with a rigid format, where uniformity and surveillance were central. Over time, uniform wooden benches, often shared, became standard across the education system, persisting in both government and private institutions even after independence.

Today, progressive and international schools are rethinking this model. Drawing inspiration from global best practices and child psychology, some are adopting flexible designs such as beanbags, mats, and modular desks, returning to an older, more intuitive understanding of how children learn best. This revival suggests that the wisdom of pre-colonial educational spaces is being acknowledged anew, blending tradition with modern research to create classrooms that respect both physical and emotional development.

India’s climatic realities

India’s predominantly tropical climate adds another layer of concern. Hot and humid conditions, combined with tightly packed benches, result in sweaty, unhygienic, and uncomfortable classroom experiences. Ceiling fans, where available, offer only limited relief. In many schools, students spend hours in close physical proximity without adequate ventilation or cooling, which not only breeds discomfort but also raises health concerns.

Children are often forced into each other’s intimate space, creating stress and distraction that detracts from learning. In such cramped and warm environments, tempers fray easily, and even minor interpersonal issues can escalate. For a country with such climate challenges, continuing with tightly packed bench seating disregards not just thermal comfort, but also basic physical and psychological wellbeing.

Responsive classroom design must consider climatic realities as central, not peripheral, to student welfare.

Psychological consequences of ignoring personal space

Psychologically, personal space is essential for healthy development. It allows children to build self-awareness, regulate emotions, and feel secure. A classroom without personal space to move or claim personal boundaries leads to chronic discomfort, anxiety, and distraction (Altman, I., 1975. The Environment and Social Behavior). Some children internalize this discomfort, resulting in low self-esteem, while others may act out through aggression or bullying.

These subtle yet powerful effects carry over into adulthood. In a culture where space is rarely respected from a young age, it is common to see adults stand in close proximity in queues or crowded public areas. Awareness should be cultivated to ensure we respect other people's personal space, and that they respect ours. This is not merely cultural; it stems from a lack of early lessons on respecting physical boundaries.

Institutional oversights and the absence of ergonomic norms

India’s educational regulators, including UGC, AICTE, NCERT, CBSE, and the state boards, define minimum infrastructure standards such as classroom size, number of toilets, drinking water, playgrounds, libraries, and labs. However, none standardize or mandate the design or dimensions of classroom seating. This oversight leaves decisions to individual schools, many of which choose shared benches for cost and space efficiency.

Even in professional colleges, this outdated model persists. The irony is hard to miss. Students are reprimanded for being too close in hallways or corridors, yet are expected to sit in constant proximity within classrooms.

In a country where innumerable schools continue to lack basic infrastructure, discussions on providing individual seating for students may seem far-fetched. But, the issue at hand is not the under-resourced rural school. It is the thousands of schools in tier 1 and tier 2 cities, including well-funded government and private institutions, where shared benches remain the norm. Change must start where it is feasible and where it can model better practices for others.

Designing for the future, not just the budget

Curriculum reforms and digital literacy have made headlines, but classroom design remains overlooked. Individual seating is not merely about ergonomics. It teaches children that their space matters, that their comfort and agency are acknowledged. Transitioning may demand planning and resources, but it pays off in improved concentration, emotional wellbeing, and student behaviour.

Enabling each student ownership of their seat also reduces vandalism and nurtures responsibility. Though chairs may require higher investment and maintenance, they offer dignity, boundaries, and an early understanding of consent and respect. These are not costs but investments in a healthier learning culture.

No single reform will solve every issue, but this is a crucial link in a larger chain. In reimagining the future of learning, we must look not only at what we teach, but where and how we ask children to learn.

Let us not forget the bench we forgot to question.

Vivek Prakasam is an architect and project management professional with over a decade of experience in designing and delivering institutional and hospitality projects across India. His work explores the intersection of built environments, human behaviour, and social impact.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Vivek Prakasam
This article turns a familiar classroom fixture – the shared wooden bench – into a powerful lens on student well-being. Rooted in colonial ideas of control, this cramped seating ignores children’s need for personal space, dignity, and comfort, especially in India’s climate. In contrast to the flexible layouts of gurukuls and madrasas, rigid benches can heighten stress, anxiety, and behavioral issues. Prakasam makes a compelling case for individual, ergonomic seating – not as a luxury, but as a foundation for healthier learning, respect for boundaries, and emotionally secure classrooms.

item.title: Caste in the classroom
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nimesh Ved

Meeting the Savarnas by Ravikant Kisana resonated in a manner than not many books do. It reminded me of some of the conversations I have had, took me back to some situations I had been a part of, prompted me to reread some sections, got me talking excitedly about the subject with friends, and make notes.

This is a book school teachers would do well to read. It may help broaden their horizons, and in turn, benefit children in the long run.

I will briefly talk about the book and follow it up with how it can help teachers.

First, the book.

This is a book that questions the credibility of the elites by pointing out how deeply entrenched caste, and as a corollary, privilege is in our society. How we have looked at, and continue to look at, privileges as the norm, as our right, or as fate. It talks about how people – public figures of the day – have only underscored the characteristics of caste and does not shy away from taking names. The author is a teacher who has a finger on the pulse of the youth. He brings out personal experiences and angst without beating around the bush and some of the lines hit hard, “We are left watching a youth that is slowly hollowing out from inside, right before our eyes.”

Then, the reasons to read it.

History. “It is a thing of the past,” a friend was fond of saying whenever caste came up in our conversations; she could not have been more uninformed. Caste continues to be omnipresent in society, and with religious fervour gaining more ground, it has only become more prominent. AI, the darling of the day, too reinforces caste. AI does not discriminate, but the data that is fed to AI is biased, and so are AI’s outputs. And, contrary to what many of us would like to believe, neither education nor urbanization have had any impact on caste. The book puts this across – loud and clear.

Conversation. Schools have begun engaging with gender and sexuality but have stayed away from talking about caste. Caste continues to be a taboo and this has not helped. Schools need to look at caste with empathy and sensitivity, bring it in day-to-day conversations, and act on what they preach. They can learn from the mistakes of the academics and patronizers, and avoid taking the higher ground. This book can play the role of a catalyst or an instigator for schools to open up and engage with caste.

Comfort zone. Increasingly, all of us are keen to stay within our comfort zones; we steer clear of all that can push our boundaries. This book goes beyond nudging; it shakes and troubles us. As G Sampath succinctly conveyed in his review (of the book) in The Hindu, “No reader, whether from above or below the glass floor, can get through these pages without multiple moments of discomfiting self-recognition.” Every once in a while, it will do teachers good to get uncomfortable, to go beyond textbooks and timetables.

Bias. Many a time, we assume that teachers are superior human beings, forgetting that they are a part of the same society and carry its DNA. Teachers are human beings, and hence, like others of their species, are biased. A majority of the teachers in our schools (and colleges) are from the upper castes, and this is where it gets tricky! It was not uncommon to hear statements such as, ‘tum log nahi sudhroge’ (you people will not change) or ‘tumhara kuch nahin ho skata’ (nothing good can become of you) on the educational campus I used to work at. Needless to say, it was the students from the marginalized castes who bore the brunt of such statements. The book reminds us to be cognisant of the biases we carry. Of the need to introspect.

Privilege. A few years ago, I was chatting with a teacher whose son went to a boarding school, where the annual fee was around 7.5 lakh rupees. She got offended when I referred to the school as elite. And she taught economics! The author’s observations on privilege are stark; he says of adolescents, “They frequently heard not just from their parents, but also from teachers, family elders and various other adult figures that they’re spoilt and soft.” Friends who teach the well-heeled elsewhere too have raised concerns on how such privilege can cause a major decline in the intellect and behaviour of the children. The first step perhaps is to recognize and accept the privilege.

Worlds. This book underscores the overlap between caste and class, and brings out how the networks amongst the privileged castes (who are a minority in our country) are so intricate and deep-rooted that the marginalized castes (the majority) can never comprehend. More importantly, it tells us how the privileged castes are unaware of the world of the marginalized castes and vice-versa. One challenge, today, as inequality in society continues to rise, is that of our worlds getting more insular, i.e., we have no idea of worlds other than our own. This book points us to the pitfalls of living on our islands and shows us worlds other than ours.

Mediocrity. This book highlights the mediocrity of the upper castes. It explains how the entire society bears the brunt of this mediocrity, and yet no one is held accountable. Mediocrity as Arthur Conan Doyle stated, ‘knows nothing higher than itself’. On the campus (mentioned above), I observed how schools are no exception; the upper caste used to act with arrogance – arrogance driven by stupidity and ignorance. They took immense pride in their mediocrity and refused to look beyond. And that is not the only school stuck in this rut.

If nothing else, the book will make teachers aware of the diversity within our society and of the opinions and views different from and similar to theirs.Meet the Savarna’s: Indian Millennials Whose Mediocrity Broke Everything

References

AI @ The Print: https://theprint.in/opinion/ai-is-learning-caste-bias-in-india/2744333/

Book Review @Hindu: https://www.thehindu.com/books/looking-through-glass-review-of-meet-the-savarnas-by-ravikant-kisana/article69892340.ece

Publisher: https://www.penguin.co.in/book/meet-the-savarnas/

Nimesh Ved currently works at a boarding school where the privileged send their children. He blogs at https://nimesh-ved.blogspot.com/ and can be reached at nimesh.explore@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nimesh Ved
Nimesh Ved’s “Caste in the Classroom” is a compelling call for educators to confront an uncomfortable truth: caste is not history – it is lived reality. Urging teachers to read Ravikant Kisana’s Meeting the Savarnas, Ved exposes how caste quietly shapes classrooms, institutions, and even modern technologies like AI. He challenges the myth that education or urban life dissolves caste, and instead invites teachers into honest, empathetic dialogue. By examining privilege, bias, and the sheltered world of the upper castes, the article positions the book as a powerful mirror – and a necessary starting point for more just, inclusive education.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: <iframe title="Meet the Savarnas: Indian Millennials Whose Mediocrity Broke Everything" type="text/html" width="500" height="550" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen style="max-width:100%" src="https://read.amazon.in/kp/card?preview=inline&linkCode=kpd&ref_=k4w_oembed_g0LvkHxjE2klQr&asin=0143465716&tag=kpembed-20"></iframe>

item.title: Schools in India today
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Nimesh Ved

Today, the economic disparity in our society is abysmally high and this disparity is only increasing. Not only does the top 1% of India’s population own more than 40% of its wealth but this 1% has also seen its wealth rise by more than 50% during recent decades. All of this has been written enough to sound cliched but unfortunately this is the story of our times.

So, how are the schools placed amidst all this?

One way to look at the schools is the boards they are affiliated to. Broadly, we have the IB, IGCSE (Cambridge) and ICSE schools at the top, read - the most expensive schools. Then, somewhere in the middle are the CBSE schools. And, at the bottom of the pyramid lie the state board schools, including government run schools. The scenario becomes stark when we talk numbers. The total number of IB, IGCSE (Cambridge) and ICSE schools, all put together, is less than 5,000; while the total number of schools in India is around 15,00,000.

Fees the schools charge or the goodwill they harbour are not necessarily driven by the boards. However, schools the children attend are not just influenced but are largely determined by their parents’ wealth. And, these numbers mirror the inequalities in our society.  

These schools for the elite are miles ahead of the schools where the economically disadvantaged study, be it the infrastructure i.e. the classrooms, subject laboratories, internet paraphernalia, and sports facilities or otherwise i.e., the range of subjects offered, number of qualified teachers and opportunities for exposure.

The differences, though, lie deeper.

For the elite, the small minority,  schools are increasingly like any other product or service they purchase. Not very different, for example, from the restaurants they frequent - where they ‘order’ and ‘pay’ for what they desire, ‘consume’ what they then feel like, and nonchalantly ‘dispose’ the rest. These schools too increasingly treat parents as ‘clients’ and many a time accept the parents’ unreasonable demands to retain them. Not surprising then that they often end up like service providers who over-sell and underperform. On top of this, for many of these parents, the schools their children go to is a matter of pride, a bragging right at the parties they attend.

For the economically disadvantaged, the clear majority, the scenario is different - they retain some modicum of respect for the schools. They look up to the schools, as the schools hold the promise of a better future for them. And children in these schools still have a spark in their eyes - a hunger for learning. Though many of them also attend schools to escape from their day-to-day lives - lives that are not exactly pleasant. Teachers, in these schools, have an upper hand and it is not uncommon to see them misuse it while the parents believe that schools will miraculously help them rise up the socio-economic ladder.

Today, not only do the children from different economic classes attend different schools but schools have different meanings for different economic classes. This is yet another step that will make one part of the society oblivious to the existence of the other, yet another reminder that we as a society are going down a slippery slope.

Nimesh follows two religions - travel and food; he blogs at https://nimesh-ved.blogspot.com/ and can be reached at  nimesh.explore@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nimesh Ved
Indian schools function within the same widening economic divide that shapes the rest of our society. In a sense, they perpetuate the wealth gap in our society as elite institutions offer world-class infrastructure and opportunities, and millions of students attend schools with far fewer resources. This piece explores how wealth determines not just access to schools, but also what schools mean to different communities.

item.title: Everything except teaching
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Shravya Mallavarapu and Ravi Kanth M

Teachers are often described as being “at the heart of the education system.” Every discussion about India’s development, whether it concerns social inequality, economic growth, or civic values, eventually returns to education. And at the centre of the education system stands the teacher. At the same time, teachers are frequently called upon to explain poor learning outcomes, particularly in government schools. When children struggle to read, write, or perform basic arithmetic, attention quickly turns to questions of teacher effort, competence, motivation, or accountability. This narrative is now familiar and deeply embedded in public discourse.

Yet, it is equally evident that over the past two decades, governments have launched multiple programmes, introduced new teaching-learning materials and organized repeated rounds of teacher training, all with the stated aim of improving classroom practice and learning levels. Despite this sustained reform effort, learning outcomes have remained stubbornly low. Large-scale assessments across states continue to show that many children in government schools are unable to meet basic learning benchmarks. This persistent gap between intent and outcome raises a difficult question – if programmes, materials, and training have been rolled out repeatedly and with genuine effort, why does meaningful change in classrooms remain so elusive?

This article, the first in a series of two, draws on a recent study conducted across government schools in Telangana that sought to examine this question from a perspective often missing in reform debates – that of the teachers themselves. Rather than begin with assumptions about what teachers lack or fail to do, the study deliberately positioned them as knowledge-holders of the system they work within. It was guided by a few simple but important principles that most teachers are trying to do their jobs under difficult conditions; that their constraints are real rather than incidental; and that understanding teaching requires listening carefully to how teachers make sense of their everyday work.

Through surveys and focus group discussions across districts, the study explored how teachers experience their daily routines, how they perceive the systems and reforms around them, and what they believe enables or hinders meaningful teaching and learning. The findings resulted in identifying a set of basic structural challenges that shape not only classroom practice but also how the teaching profession itself is understood and valued. This article closely examines one such challenge of the steady expansion of non-teaching responsibilities of teachers and the organization of their working day around this expectation.

What fills a teacher’s day

Across discussions, teachers consistently described how a large part of their working day is consumed by activities that have little to do with teaching or learning. While school is expected to be centred on children’s learning and development, much of the teacher’s time is instead claimed by clerical, administrative, and departmental tasks. Many estimated that, on most days, they are able to spend barely three hours actually teaching, often broken into short stretches of 15 to 20 minutes. In effect, more than half the working day is taken up by non-teaching responsibilities.

In government schools across Telangana, the first instructional period is typically spent recording attendance through a Facial Recognition based mobile application while also maintaining entries in physical registers. The process is slow, particularly in areas with unstable connectivity, often taking 30 to 40 minutes and requiring completion within a fixed time window. Delays are followed by calls from administrative offices seeking immediate updates. This is followed by supervision of preparation of ragi java and the mid-day meal, along with detailed record-keeping in both online and physical formats. By the time these processes are completed, much of the first half of the school day has already passed, leaving very little sustained teaching time before lunch.

Beyond the morning routine, teachers manage a wide range of administrative tasks throughout the day. They upload assessment and learning data across multiple portals, respond to ad-hoc requests from different administrative levels and repeatedly enter the same information in different formats. They coordinate school events, manage records for uniforms and textbooks, organize parent-teacher meetings, liaise with donors and CSR partners and prepare documentation for official visits. In schools without adequate support staff, teachers also ensure that classrooms and toilets are cleaned and arrange drinking water when required. Election duties, surveys, census work, health campaigns and awareness drives further fill up the calendar.

Individually, many of these responsibilities appear minor or seem to be undertaking only occasionally. But when added up, they leave little uninterrupted time for teaching. Teachers described their day as a continuous movement between administrative and classroom tasks. Many expressed frustration, noting that duties peripheral to education have come to define their routine. The absence of clear boundaries between academic and non-academic responsibilities has normalized the diversion of their time and reduced their control over the workday.

List of non-teaching tasks reported by teachers

When teaching is no longer the core of the profession

The consequences of this reorganization of work extend beyond the loss of instructional time. Over time, it changes what teaching itself becomes. Teaching depends on continuity, attentiveness, and the ability to stay mentally present with learners. When the school day is structured around frequent interruptions and administrative demands, teaching is gradually pushed to the margins, rather than forming the core of the day.

Teachers are often told that they enjoy autonomy in their classrooms and that no one dictates exactly how they should teach. Many administrators genuinely believe this. Yet, teachers repeatedly described feeling constrained and unsettled within their classrooms. The difference lies between theoretical freedom and practical possibility. A teacher may be free in principle to plan and adapt instruction, but meaningful professional judgement requires time, stability, and sustained engagement with students. When the day is fragmented by non-teaching responsibilities, that space simply does not exist. As mental energy is absorbed by peripheral tasks, teachers rely more on rote methods or textbook reading just to manage the class.
