The continuous effort of switching between administrative and instructional roles leaves little room for reflection or adaptation, and teaching becomes mechanical.

The teachers we spoke to fully recognized this shift. When asked to describe what their professional work should involve, they spoke of planning lessons, identifying learning gaps, adapting instruction, and attending to students’ academic and emotional needs. Yet, when they described their daily routines, these activities occupied very little of their time. Many expressed discomfort at the gap between what teaching ought to mean and what their work had come to consist of. Even newly recruited teachers reported feeling disoriented within months of joining service.

Gradually, the profession is redefined in practice. Over time, teachers come to see themselves – and are seen by others – not primarily as educators, but as multi-purpose functionaries responsible for keeping the administrative machinery of the system running.

Restoring teaching as the core work*

Debates on poor learning outcomes often return to teacher motivation, effort, or accountability. But teachers’ accounts suggest something more basic. Before asking how well teachers teach, we must ask whether the structure of the workday allows teaching to remain the central activity. This pattern is not limited to one state. Across India, similar concerns have surfaced repeatedly. Teachers in multiple states such as Gujarat, Maharashtra, Haryana, Andhra Pradesh, and West Bengal protested being assigned non-teaching work – from census and vaccination drives to scholarship and Aadhaar data entry – arguing that it diverted time and focus from classrooms. National studies reinforce what these incidents suggest. An analysis by NIEPA (National Institute of Educational Planning and Administration) found that teachers spent only about 19% of their time on direct teaching, with the rest absorbed by school management and non-teaching work. A Delhi government study similarly reported that less than half of teachers’ time was spent on instruction, with most hours taken up by documentation and administrative compliance. In both cases, teachers identified non-academic duties as the single largest barrier to their professional work.

The Right to Education (RTE) Act already explicitly exempts teachers from such non-teaching duties, except for deployment for limited civic functions such as census or election work. As stated in Chapter IV, Clause 27 of the Act, “No teacher shall be deployed for any non-educational purposes other than the decennial population census, disaster relief duties or duties relating to elections to the local authority or the State Legislatures or Parliament, as the case may be.” In practice, however, teachers continue to be assigned a wide range of administrative and logistical responsibilities that dilute both their right to teach and children’s right to learn. While the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 acknowledges this problem and mentions that “teachers will not be engaged any longer in work that is not directly related to teaching,” it stops short of defining what constitutes “time-consuming administrative assignments” or establishing mechanisms to ensure compliance.

Taken together, teachers’ accounts and national evidence point to an often overlooked reality that learning cannot improve where teaching itself is not structurally protected. The system’s reliance on teachers as a convenient administrative workforce violates not only legal provisions but also the moral foundation of public education. Improving learning outcomes, therefore, cannot begin with exhortations for teachers to perform better. It must begin with restoring the conditions that make teaching possible in the form of uninterrupted time, clear limits on non-teaching work and support structures that allow teachers to remain with students rather than systems.

*References

  1. https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/city/rajkot/teachers-protest-being-given-blo-duties/articleshow/124347380.cms?utm_source=chatgpt.com
  2. https://www.hindustantimes.com/cities/mumbai-news/burdened-with-mandatory-apps-school-teachers-threaten-digital-disconnect-101766518037248.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com
  3. https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/city/kolkata/bengal-school-heads-protest-ecs-blo-duty-orders/articleshow/123266533.cms?utm_source=chatgpt.com
  4. https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/city/gurgaon/haryana-education-dept-remove-teachers-from-long-term-non-academic-duties/articleshow/125623857.cms?utm_source=chatgpt.com
  5. National Institute of Educational Planning and Administration (India). (2016). Teachers in the Indian education system. New Delhi: NIEPA
  6. Accountability Initiative, & Delhi Commission for Protection of Child Rights. (2021). Study on time allocation and work perception of Delhi’s government school teachers. Centre for Policy Research.)

Note: This article, which is the first of a two-part seires, draws on the study Beyond the Classroom: Government Teachers’ Views on Poor Learning Outcomes in Telangana, commissioned by Samagra Shiksha, Department of School Education, Government of Telangana. The study was conducted by the Behavioural Insights Unit (BIU), Department of Communication, University of Hyderabad and UNICEF Telangana. The report is available on the Telangana Education Commission website. The full report can also be obtained by writing to the authors.

Shravya Mallavarapu is an education researcher with over 12 years of experience working on foundational literacy and numeracy within public education systems. She can be reached at shravya.mallavarapu@gmail.com.

Dr. Ravi Kanth is a researcher working in behavioural science and public policy. He can be reached at emailofravi@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Shravya Mallavarapu and Ravi Kanth M
India’s government school teachers are spending more time on paperwork than on pupils. From facial-recognition attendance and data portals to mid-day meal supervision and election surveys, over half their day is consumed by administrative duties – leaving barely three fragmented hours for teaching. This “multi-purpose functionary” model not only undermines the spirit of the Right to Education Act but also reduces teaching to a rushed, mechanical exercise. The result: compromised learning outcomes. Real reform, the authors argue, won’t come from more training alone – it demands restoring uninterrupted classroom time and assigning dedicated staff for non-teaching tasks.

item.title: Who is really raising India’s children?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neerja Singh

Today, many Indian children are growing up less under the watch of parents and teachers and more under the influence of screens. Online gurus, Instagram influencers, peer groups, and a flood of unfiltered content are shaping how children think, feel, and behave. What looks like harmless scrolling is clashing with the rights our Constitution guarantees them. Articles 21 and 21A, for instance, promise children a life of dignity and a meaningful education. Yet, the digital world they inhabit often pulls them in the opposite direction. The signs are hard to ignore – rising anxiety, depression, and a heart-breaking increase in teen suicides.

At the heart of this issue is a simple truth. Article 21 protects a child’s right to live with dignity. Article 21A ensures free and compulsory education focused on healthy, holistic growth. Courts have even said that protection from online harm falls within these rights, especially under laws like POCSO and the IT Act. But while the law recognizes the danger, the internet moves faster than enforcement. Digital players operate freely, and children are left exposed.

Take the curious rise of “kid-gurus.” Children barely 10 years old have hundreds of thousands of followers on social media, offering spiritual advice to adults. Accounts like Abhinav Arora’s on Instagram are often managed by parents and turned into family ventures. What gets lost is childhood itself. Playtime becomes content time. Privacy disappears. Normal growth is replaced by public performance. Our existing laws on child labour and contracts were never designed for this digital reality. Since contracts involving minors are not enforceable, there is little clarity on who protects the child’s interests or what happens to the money earned in their name.

Another worrying shift is how childhood aspirations themselves are changing. Earlier, children wanted to become teachers, doctors, scientists, artists, or athletes. Today, a surprising number say they want to become “influencers” or “YouTubers.” Fame has become a career goal even before identity is formed. Children are learning to measure their worth in views, likes, and comments rather than in skills, values, or learning. This constant need for validation creates a fragile sense of self that depends on strangers on the internet. When appreciation drops, so does self-esteem.

Parents, too, often feel caught in this tide. Many do not fully understand the platforms their children use but hesitate to restrict access for fear of appearing outdated or overly strict. Some even encourage early online presence, believing it offers opportunities. What is missing is awareness that the digital world is not designed with children’s wellbeing in mind; it is designed to maximize engagement and profit.

We must begin honest conversations at home, in schools, and in policy spaces about what healthy digital childhood looks like. Protecting children today means not only guarding them from physical harm but also shielding their minds, emotions, and identities from a world they are not yet equipped to navigate alone.

India has signed the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child, which says children must be protected from exploitation and must have a voice in matters affecting them. But in these cases, we rarely ask what the child wants or how they feel about living in constant public view. Even Sudha Murthy has spoken about the emotional burden and loss of privacy such children may carry for life. Religious pride or social admiration should never come at the cost of a child’s dignity.

Then there is the daily diet of influencer culture. Children see perfect bodies, luxury lifestyles, and promises of instant success. Algorithms push extreme content because it keeps consumers hooked. Studies in 2024 showed that more than a third of Indian teenagers report symptoms of depression and anxiety linked to social media use. Many spend four to seven hours a day scrolling. This affects their sleep, attention span, self-confidence, and even brain development. While the Digital Personal Data Protection Act relies on parental consent to protect minors online, children easily bypass age checks with false information, making these safeguards more theoretical than real.

Peer pressure makes things worse. WhatsApp groups and Snapchat chats often become silent spaces for bullying. A fake image, a rumour, or a humiliating message can spread within seconds. Victims feel trapped because the harassment follows them home. The Delhi High Court cases* under Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 and the Information Technology Act, 2000 show that online harassment is taken seriously, yet many offenders hide behind anonymity. The National Crime Records Bureau data showing a sharp rise in student suicides over recent years reflects how digital stress and academic pressure are combining into a dangerous mix.

At the same time, children stumble upon content no child should see: pornography, violent ideologies, and misinformation. The Supreme Court has made it clear that even viewing child sexual content is illegal, yet access remains easy. In some parts of India, studies show that very young children exceed safe screen-time limits, leading to developmental delays that worry doctors and parents alike. Teenagers, meanwhile, can be drawn into online echo chambers that slowly radicalize or groom them without anyone noticing.

The consequences show up quietly at home and in classrooms. Children become anxious, withdrawn, easily distracted, and unsure of themselves. Some struggle with identity when early fame fades. Others suffer silently from bullying or exposure to harmful ideas. Families are left dealing with emotional damage caused by sources that are impossible to hold accountable.

Our laws are trying to catch up but are still behind. The Supreme Court recently declined to ban under-13s from social media, saying parental consent should be enough. But we know how easily children work around such rules. Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 and the Information Technology Act, 2000, punish those who share illegal content, but there are no clear rules for child influencers, digital child labour, or peer-driven online harm. The courts have repeatedly reminded us that faith, fame, or profit cannot override a child’s right to dignity.

So where do we go from here? We need to start treating child influencing as digital labour that requires oversight. Earnings should be protected in trusts. Psychological wellbeing must be monitored. Countries like the United States and France already have laws for child performers and influencers that India can learn from. Schools need to teach digital literacy in practical ways. Parents need tools and guidance to manage screen time and online exposure. And stronger action is needed against those who exploit children through content farms, bullying networks, or radical messaging.

If we do not act soon, we risk raising a generation shaped more by algorithms than by care, education, and human connection. Protecting children in the digital age is not just good parenting, it is a constitutional responsibility.

*https://www.scconline.com/blog/post/2025/08/06/safer-digital-spaces-for-children-delhi-hc-pocso-cyberbullying-case/

https://www.thehindu.com/news/cities/Delhi/delhi-hc-to-review-trial-court-verdict-on-viewing-and-circulating-child-pornography/article68175060.ece

Neerja Singh is a generational diversity speaker, author of six generational books, and a generational coach. A TEDx speaker and certified virtual presenter, she assists multinational companies, educational institutions, and the armed forces to bridge generational gaps. An Oxford University Press Resource Person, she is pursuing a Senior Management Executive Program at IIM Trichy. She can be reached at https://www.linkedin.com/in/neerja-singh/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neerja Singh
Screens and influencers are fast replacing parents and teachers, reshaping childhood in troubling ways. As “kid-gurus” rise and YouTube fame becomes a dream career, children’s self-worth is increasingly tied to clicks and likes, often sacrificing privacy, dignity, and healthy development. Neerja Singh talks about the psychological costs of influencer culture – anxiety, cyberbullying, depression, even suicide – and argues that current laws lag behind digital realities. She calls for urgent regulation, stronger digital literacy, and collective responsibility to protect children’s constitutional right to grow up safe, whole, and human.

item.title: WhatsApp voice notes: my unexpected teaching tool
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Salini Krishnan

It started, like many meaningful things, with a message. A student reached out, politely and shyly, explaining his struggle with spoken English. He had an uncertain voice and the kind of hesitation that comes not just from language gaps, but from years of feeling unheard or unsure.

I recognized that tone immediately. I’ve heard it in so many learners who are far more fluent in their thoughts than in their words. He wasn’t asking for perfection – just confidence. Just enough skill to speak without anxious pangs or crippling doubt.

In a typical classroom environment, we would have sat face-to-face. I would’ve observed his body language, listened between the pauses, adjusted pace and tone in real time. But we didn’t have that luxury. He had moved on to semester 2, where he did not have English classes; his schedule and mine did not match anymore. And the pandemic had left a lasting loathing of Zoom calls and Teams meetings. Neither of us wanted to revisit that experience.

So, I did something I’d never done before: I opened WhatsApp and pressed the mic icon and delivered some specific instructions: “Hi! Let’s give this a shot!” I said, sending my first voice note, “Tell me about your day in English. Don’t stress about sounding correct. Just speak to me freely. I’ll listen and respond.”

The choice wasn’t particularly strategic at first. It was merely convenient. But over time, I began to see something deeper: the pedagogical value of voice-based, asynchronous dialogue. Firstly, unlike text, voice carries warmth. It reveals emotion, hesitation, and personality. Unlike video, it strips away visual self-consciousness – no worries about how your hair looks, or what your background reveals. You can speak while splayed on a bean bag or walking your dog.

My student quickly confessed that this was liberating. He could record, pause, delete, retry. There was no pressure of immediate response, no spotlight. I wasn’t hovering over his shoulder correcting every mistake. Instead, I was a voice in his pocket – easily accessible, quietly encouraging, and comfortably paced.

What began as Plan B, quickly became a method. Each day, he sent me a short recording. Sometimes it was about a film or a Netflix series he had seen. Sometimes he tried to retell a news article in his own words. More than once, he shared a YouTube video he had found interesting, and we’d proceed to have a discussion of the content and the linguistic features that made it appealing. On one occasion, he even sent a recording explaining why he couldn’t send a recording the previous day! I’d reply with observations on everything from his pace and complexity of sentence structure to the inevitable trappings of mother tongue influence and the chaotic rules of sentence stress. I would also model speech. I’d say the same sentence the way a native speaker might. I’d vary intonation, break up complex words, exaggerate stress. It became a kind of audio duet – call and response, creation and correction.

Initially, his messages were short, stilted, cautious. He paused every few words. The recordings were long but littered with long bouts of silence and heavy breathing. Almost all of them ended abruptly without a proper conclusion.

By week three, his sentences were longer. He was improvising, experimenting with content and styles, using humorours idioms – badly, sometimes hilariously, but bravely. He not only used fewer filler words but was quick to correct me if I slipped into an extended ‘errr’. He even attempted sarcasm, which I consider to be the Ph.D of second-language skills.

I remember one voice note clearly: he described a conversation with a friend who had approached him for help to prepare for an interview. He spoke calmly and with clarity. He told me in his recording that day, “I didn’t panic. I just thought – what would you say?” That moment hit me hard. Because I realized that in helping him hear himself, I had become a kind of internal reference voice – a tool he could carry beyond our chats.

After he graduated and the ‘classes’ concluded, I found myself dissecting this unexpected method that had served us so well. What I realized was this: voice recordings activated several key principles of good language learning.

  • Repetition – He could repeat my models as many times as needed. No one was watching. No one was interrupting. Mistakes didn’t feel like failures – they felt like re-tries.
  • High accuracy feedback – I had the chance to listen carefully and give thoughtful, precise feedback, rather than reacting in the moment.
  • Listening as immersion – My voice notes weren’t just corrections. They were a consistent stream of real spoken English, tailored to his level, and attuned to his capability.
  • Self-learning – As he re-listened to his own recordings, he could hear things he missed the first time: dropped articles, mispronounced words, awkward phrasing. His ability to detect mistakes and his awareness grew significantly.

Here are my reflections on the experience as a teacher. This experience reminded me that teaching doesn’t always require a syllabus. Sometimes, it’s just presence and a willingness to meet the learner where they are. I used to believe that effective language instruction needed face- to-face interaction. And yes, that’s ideal. But it’s not the only way.

The method is not without its challenges. I had to be crystal clear in my instructions. I had to predict the stumbling blocks and build my explanations into the recording itself. There was no whiteboard or visual cues to fall back on. No facial cues to guide me. But with an invested learner, anything is possible.

Recently, this student reached out (on WhatsApp!) to thank me. He said, “Now I don’t stress about speaking. I just start.” That’s the win. Not perfect English, not accent reduction. Just starting. It made me think of the countless learners out there who avoid speaking because they fear embarrassment, who think fluency is some distant mountain peak instead of a daily walk. This method gave my student daily practice without the pressure. It made the mountain scalable.

Salini Krishnan is a faculty member in the English Department at Anglo-Eastern Maritime Academy, Karjat, Maharashtra. She has nearly a decade of experience in the field of ESL and is an Internationally certified TESOL instructor. Her domains of professional interests include Business English, Pragmatics in Professional Communication and Intercultural and Gender-sensitive workplace communication. She can be reached at Krishnan.S@angloeastern.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Salini Krishnan
This article reframes WhatsApp voice notes as a quietly powerful pedagogical tool for hesitant ESL speakers. Born from scheduling constraints and digital burnout, this low-pressure, camera-free practice created space for risk-taking without fear of judgment. Through a daily exchange of recorded speech and targeted audio feedback, the learner absorbed modelled intonation, structure, and rhythm at their own pace – pausing, replaying, and self-correcting in private. The result was more than improved fluency: the teacher’s voice became an internal guide, enabling the learner to replace anxiety with confidence, humour, and experimentation, and to see spoken English as a habit built daily rather than as a distant ideal.

item.title: Breaking the attention deficit: a hands-on reset for the digital generation
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Anuradha C

Walk into any classroom today and you’ll see it – the restless shifting, the quick loss of focus, the need for constant stimulation. Excessive screen time has clearly rewired attention patterns, but repeating that diagnosis doesn’t help teachers or schools.

What does help is a reset.

Interestingly, many students instinctively understand this idea through anime. Long before anime became a digital streaming phenomenon, it was a deeply physical art form – hand-drawn frames, painstakingly animated scenes, repeated sketches that demanded patience, focus, and craft. Ironically, stories that now play on glowing screens were born from creators who worked entirely with paper, pencils, models, and movement. That evolution itself offers a powerful analogy for education today: attention is not lost, it is trained.

This article is not about banning devices or moralizing screen time. It’s about helping schools and teachers design learning experiences that naturally pull students back into focus – using their hands, bodies, and social instincts.

Think of it less as restriction and more as a training arc.

1. Put the hands back into learning

Attention improves dramatically when the body is involved. This isn’t a trend – it’s neuroscience. When hands move, the brain follows.

What schools can do

Make “do with your hands” activities a core part of the curriculum, not an occasional enrichment exercise.

Practical, doable ideas:

  • Modelling and making: Clay models for cells, molecules, landforms. Cardboard prototyping for physics or design concepts. Origami for geometry and fractions.
  • Gardening as learning: Measuring plant growth, tracking sunlight, testing soil, maintaining observation journals. Science, math, and responsibility – rooted in reality.
  • Reverse engineering sessions: Children can be encouraged to safely tinker with discarded items like old fans, radios, toys, or keyboards. Doing so can teach them to understand components and label components, sketch circuits, and attempt reassembly. Because curiosity matters more than success.
  • Repair culture: Fix a loose hinge, stitch a torn bag, rewire a plug (safely, age-appropriate). Repair builds patience and pride.

If this sounds familiar, it should. In Fullmetal Alchemist (a manga series), understanding comes from breaking things down to their fundamentals. There’s no shortcut to mastery – only engagement.

2. Redesign homework: less screen time, more sense

Homework often unintentionally extends screen time. It doesn’t have to.

Device-free homework that still builds rigor

  • Observation-based tasks: Track shadows across a day. Sketch cloud patterns. Measure water usage at home.
  • Conversation homework: Interview parents or grandparents about how they learned math, science, or problem-solving.
  • Build challenges: Design a paper bridge, a marble run, or a water filter using household materials.
  • Analog research: Use physical books, newspapers, handwritten notes, or teacher-provided material.

The goal is not to consume more information – it’s to train attention, curiosity, and reflection.

3. Exams that test thinking, not typing

When exams rely heavily on devices, students optimize for speed, shortcuts, and surface recall.

Alternatives that work:

  • Handwritten open-book exams that reward synthesis over memorization.
  • Practical assessments: Explain a concept using a model, diagram, or physical demonstration.
  • Viva-style evaluations: Short conversations where students explain how they think.
  • Group problem-solving tests: Assess reasoning, collaboration, and communication.

Real-world problems don’t arrive neatly formatted. Exams shouldn’t either.

4. Engineer social interaction (don’t leave it to chance)

Attention strengthens when students share focus with others – but social skills need structure.

How schools can design this intentionally

  • Daily or weekly discussion circles: Low-pressure spaces to speak, listen, and reflect.
  • Team-based challenges: Build something together. Solve a local problem together.
  • Rotating leadership roles: Planner, presenter, note-taker, facilitator – everyone participates.
  • Visible rewards for collaboration: Celebrate teamwork, empathy, and peer support – not just individual scores.

Think Haikyuu!! (a manga series) Raw talent matters, but real growth happens when players learn to read each other, synchronize, and trust the team.

5. Getting buy-in from students (the make-or-break factor)

If students feel this is adults “fixing” them, resistance is guaranteed.

How to bring students along

  • Explain the why clearly: “We’re training your focus like a skill – just like stamina or strength.”
  • Make progress visible: Track focus time, project completion, or collaboration milestones.
  • Offer choice: Let students select materials, themes, or project formats.
  • Gamify attention: Focus streaks, maker badges, teamwork levels.

Anime fans understand training arcs. No one starts overpowered. Even Naruto (a popular manga series) had to work relentlessly on focus, discipline, and repetition before levelling up.

6. Parents: Lead by example, not instructions

School efforts succeed only if home environments support them.

What parents can realistically do:

  • Put phones away during meals and conversations.
  • Create analog rituals – board games, cooking, walks, fixing things together.
  • Set predictable device times instead of constant access.
  • Allow boredom. That’s where imagination begins.

Children absorb behaviour far more than advice.

From control to capability

This is not about controlling students or demonizing technology. It’s about restoring attention as a capability.

When students build, touch, repair, collaborate, and reflect, focus returns naturally – without force.

Schools don’t need massive budgets or radical reforms. They need intent, consistency, and trust in hands-on learning.

Because attention isn’t gone. It’s just waiting for the right training arc to begin.

Anuradha C is an IT industry drop-out after several years of slogging and money-making. She is now working freelance as a corporate technical trainer and content writer. She is hoping to channelize her passion for writing into a satisfying experience for herself and a joyous experience for her readers. She can be reached at anuradhac@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anuradha C
In an age of shrinking attention spans, this compelling piece calls for a bold educational “reset.” Instead of doubling down on screens, it champions hands-on learning – gardening, building, repairing – as a powerful way to retrain focus and restore cognitive stamina. Drawing inspiration from the disciplined training arcs of popular anime heroes, it argues that concentration isn’t lost – it’s built. By reimagining homework, exams, and classroom culture around analogue problem-solving and real-world collaboration, the article urges educators and parents to unite in modelling meaningful, offline engagement for the digital generation.

item.title: Nurturing minds and hearts: Delhi’s Happiness Curriculum in action
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Mansi Nanda, Jwalin Patel, Seema Nath, and Murari Jha

The interaction between humans and technology appears to be entering a very distinct phase, one where experts believe that technology may go beyond human control and potentially start controlling humans. With so many machines and gadgets surrounding us, life has become mechanized, leaving little or no time for individuals to think and feel. This creates a situation where people can easily be controlled and manipulated – an ideal scenario for the neoliberal world economy, which views individuals as consumers who should constantly consume more. This trend is not only dehumanizing but also putting life on this planet at great risk, as many climate activists believe. In fact, this concern has moved beyond belief, as there is strong evidence supporting climate change, which could have catastrophic consequences for human life on Earth.

In this neoliberal, market-driven economy, what is rapidly depleting is the individual’s connection with themselves and with others. Core values such as empathy, care, and compassion – values that are essential to holding civilizations together – are in decline. If we want to restore life on our planet, we must restore these core values in individuals. It is rightly said that values are not taught but caught, and this was more applicable when our lives were structured around strong social fabrics. The fast-paced model of city life is fundamentally different from the communal life that earlier generations experienced in villages. Most of the important lessons that children used to learn from their communities are no longer easily available. Thus, schools, which still shelter children for a significant number of years, must now purposefully cultivate values like love, care, and compassion.

People working to restore human values to save the planet believe that schools can indeed achieve this. Over the past decade, this idea has evolved into a global movement. Various governments and private institutions have introduced Social and Emotional Learning (SEL) curricula in their education systems. In India, the introduction of the Happiness Curriculum in Delhi schools, inspired by the philosophy of A Nagraj, draws particular attention. In Delhi, a research study conducted by researchers at THRIvE Research Centre explored the perceptions of children and teachers about happiness.

The curriculum was implemented across 1,030 Delhi government schools in 2019 and was inaugurated by His Holiness The 14th Dalai Lama at an event organized by the Delhi government. The curriculum was to be taught from kindergarten to grade 8. Speaking at the launch event, Manish Sisodia, the architect of the curriculum and former deputy chief minister of Delhi, said, "This is a 10-year-long curriculum, and we believe that after 10 years, a child exposed to this will be fundamentally different – a person who cares, a person with empathy and compassion, someone who can understand, regulate, and express emotions, someone embedded in the community, and who thinks about the betterment of the community and the nation."

The curriculum was built around four pillars: daily mindfulness practice, storytelling, activities, and expression day. The aim was to ensure that each child engages in reflective practices. For instance, a teacher might take a month to teach a story – not rushing to complete the syllabus but ensuring that every child has the opportunity to reflect on how they relate to the story. Similarly, children were encouraged to share how they felt after mindfulness practice once a week.

There are strong psychological and philosophical underpinnings behind these practices. Agrahar Nagraj, a famous Indian philosopher and scholar, introduced a conceptual model to understand and cultivate long term wellbeing. According to this model, human life is a combination of four dimensions – work, behaviour, thought, and experience. Our sensations, emotions, understanding, and awareness are connected to these dimensions. If we combine these dimensions, they form a happiness triad (Nagraj, 1999). In other words, living in all these dimensions leads to a constant expectation of fulfilment in humans. This fulfilment manifests as peace and contentment. This is happiness. His philosophy served as a foundation for the happiness curriculum. Historian Yuval Noah Harari, author of Sapiens, often states how mindfulness practices have transformed his life. He explains that after his PhD from Oxford, he realized he knew much about the world around him but very little about what goes on inside his own mind. He discovered that mindfulness is one of the best tools to gain control and understanding of the mind. ThichNhatHanh, the renowned Buddhist teacher and author of The Miracle of Mindfulness, argues that mindfulness is the only way to truly live in the present. If we are unable to live fully in the present, we will never be able to live our future.

The stories told in the happiness classes were built around important values such as understanding our own needs. For instance, there was a story in the 7th-grade book, Kitna Bada Ghera. The story narrates how a king once offered to allocate as much land as one could encircle. Everyone began encircling the land, but they were unhappy because they saw that others had encircled larger pieces of land. However, there was one person who didn’t encircle any land; instead, he simply built his hut and started living. When the king asked him why, he replied, "This is what I need; I don’t need a larger piece of land." Activities within the happiness curriculum were designed similarly.

But how have students received this curriculum?

One of our research methods involved using a photovoice activity, where children were asked to take pictures that they felt closely related to their idea of happiness. They were then asked to describe why they took the picture and how it represented their happiness. Child friendly cameras were provided and children were instructed not to capture the faces of others. The children came up with a wide variety of photos and descriptions, revealing their perspectives on happiness.

Findings

Children’s perceptions of happiness can broadly be categorized into three main themes:

  1. Connectedness to self (personal awareness)

This theme includes photos and descriptions that showcase children’s self-awareness. They expressed finding opportunities to connect with themselves through activities like spending time in Nature, playing, and engaging in reflective exercises. Many children took photos of trees, describing how being in Nature helped them relax and feel calm. They saw the growth of plants as analogous to their own growth, drawing parallels between their experiences and natural processes.

2. Connectedness to others (social and interpersonal relations)

This theme includes children’s descriptions of supportive relationships in their lives, particularly with parents, teachers, and friends. Children often expressed gratitude towards their parents for their support and described building good habits to earn their appreciation. They also spoke highly of their teachers, especially those who taught happiness classes, emphasizing how these teachers were friendly, understanding, and contributed to their happiness. One of the primary reasons that inspires them to come to school regularly is their friends. They find happiness in the simple act of engaging with their friends at school.

3. Engagement in prosocial behaviours (social justice and environmental awareness)

Some children focused on the importance of environmental conservation, taking photos that highlighted saving trees, growing plants, and maintaining cleanliness. These children expressed the belief that protecting the environment is integral to their happiness and showed how they engage in activities that bring about positive change around them.

Cutting across the theme, children's notions of happiness are as varied and diverse as the concept of happiness itself. What is important to note, however, is that they are constructing their own understanding of happiness. This is significant because they are not simply memorizing a definition of happiness; instead, they are reflecting on it and trying to grasp its meaning. The larger themes that emerged from our research suggest that children’s understanding and interpretation of happiness align closely with the universal concept of happiness, which revolves around ideas of self-awareness, social awareness, and a connection to Nature.

Conclusion

Through these findings, we see that children's sense of happiness is deeply tied to self-awareness, interpersonal connections, and a commitment to making the world a better place. There can be a debate about the concept of happiness, as Will Durant states in his book The Greatest Minds and Ideas of All Time, "Nor may we define progress in terms of happiness, for idiots are happier than geniuses, and those whom we most respect seek not happiness but greatness." This comment critiques the simplistic interpretation of happiness. If we view it as one component of the broader idea of Social Emotional Learning (SEL), happiness becomes one of the most essential skills of our time. It is a connection to the self that has the power to liberate individuals from the market and technology-driven culture of consumerism, allowing them to contribute significantly to protecting the planet.

And what could be greater than realizing that we don't only live but also care for the generations who will inherit this planet from us? Social and emotional learning skills promise to prepare individuals who can pass down a safe and vibrant planet to the next generation, and this journey begins with discovering the self. In our research, we found evidence of children, exposed to an SEL-based curriculum, showing signs of self-awareness, conveying connectedness to self, others, and the larger society. These in turn, help positively shape their ideas and everyday lives, making a strong case for promoting SEL for school children.

Acknowledgements: We would like to acknowledge the support of Cell for Human Value and Transformative Learning (CHVTL)-State Council of Educational Research and Training’s (SCERT) Delhi, Dream a Dream and Mind & Life Institute. In addition, we would like to specifically thank Dr Sreehari Ravindranath, Ms Apoorva Bhatnagar, and Dr Benjamin Alcott for their support.

Mansi Nanda, Jwalin Patel, Seema Nath, and Murari Jha work at THRIvE research centre. They can be reached at research@tideinternational.org.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mansi Nanda, Jwalin Patel, Seema Nath, and Murari Jha
In 2019 the Delhi government had launched the Happiness Curriculum to rehumanize education in an increasingly market-driven world. Through mindfulness, storytelling, and reflection, the curriculum moves beyond rote learning to cultivate empathy, self-awareness, and emotional wellbeing among students from kindergarten to grade eight. Research into this curriculum by the authors reveals that children come to see happiness as rooted in inner balance, meaningful relationships, and prosocial actions like caring for the environment – showcasing how Social and Emotional Learning can empower future-ready, compassionate citizens.

item.title: Final notes in Tamarind Grove: conservation and ancient wisdom
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat

All the children and students crossed the road and approached the Gangamma temple. Muniyappa asked us to leave our footwear under the “pipale” tree before entering the temple.

“This tree looks so majestic. It's tall and wide. I love these heart-shaped leaves. See the tapering leaf tip. We can make greeting cards with these leaves. A neem tree has also grown by its side,” a student exclaimed, looking at the pipal tree.

“I have seen these trees in many temples. Why is it always found near temples with a platform built around it?” Rini asked.

“Its scientific name is Ficus religiosa. In Sanskrit, it is called ashwatha. It belongs to the Fig family. In the scriptures, the pipal is described as being the best tree as it has its roots above and branches below; it is considered to be an eternal tree. Hindus associate this tree with the three gods – Brahma (Creator), Vishnu (Preserver), and Shiva (Destroyer). The tree is believed to be Vishnu himself, and at the same time, Vishnu is said to have been born under it, and as an infant, floated on a leaf of Ficus religiosa. Krishna, the eighth incarnation of Vishnu, died sitting under this tree. This is the reason the pipal is never cut, but worshipped,” Prabhakar explained.

I added, “An infusion of its bark is used as an antidiabetic medicine. A decoction of the bark is used as a skin wash to treat scabies, ulcers, and other skin diseases. Its leaves and twigs are used as an antidote against venomous animal bites.”

“The pipal is often planted with another tree like the neem, which is also a multipurpose tree. This provides sufficient evidence of why local plants are credited as divine,” Madhavi said.

“But I thought bargad, another kind of fig tree, is called ashwatha. We saw it while we were walking to the temple. There was a bulbul sitting on it,” Sujatha said.

“I think while referring to the ancient texts, it is difficult to ascertain the species of modern taxonomy. Doubt arises as plants mentioned differ in the same Purana in different versions and contexts. Each plant has many names in Sanskrit. A plant has to be identified based on its morphological, medicinal, and mythological characteristics. The pipal tree might be attributed to the Ficus species, which also includes the bargad (banyan), the oudumbara (cluster fig), and other similar trees,” I said.

“In Buddhism, bodhi is the pipal tree, and is considered sacred because of its association with the Buddha’s enlightenment. It is also known as the ‘Tree of Life’ and is a symbol of spiritual awakening,” a student added.

“Fig trees are very important for the ecosystem. They support many birds, insects, and reptiles, and conserve soil fertility. It is called a keystone species, which means that its role in maintaining the ecosystem is crucial,” Prabhakar explained.

“Then which tree is referred to as the kalpavruksh?” a student asked.

“Kalpavruksh means wish-fulfilling tree. In the southern part of India, this label is given to the coconut tree, because we use all parts of the tree. Coconut is very important while performing any ritual. But in northern India, kalpavruksh is the mulberry tree. In the same way, shami tree may be Prosopis cineraria or Dichrostachys cinneria. Itprobably depends on the availability of the plant in that region,” Prabhakar replied.

“How did planting certain trees near the temple and worshiping them start?” a student asked.

“In many indigenous cultures, there are deities associated with natural phenomena such as the sun, moon, stars, mountains, rivers, and animals. These deities are often viewed as the manifestation of the natural world, and worshiping them is seen as a way of connecting with and honouring the natural world. Some religious traditions view nature as a reflection of god’s power and majesty, while others see it as a gift to humanity that should be respected and protected. I think our ancestors used religion as a way to protect our environment. Otherwise, if we only consider the utilitarian value, many creatures on Earth would have vanished by now. See here, you can read this book,” said Prabhakar holding out a book.

“The cover of this book looks great. What are these elephants feeding on?” a student asked.

“They are feeding on the tender shoots of a tamarind,” I replied.

“Are there elephants in this grove too?” Shalini asked.

“No, but look at those beautiful creatures,” Prabhakar pointed at a couple of peacocks.

“Peacocks! Full family is here, it's wonderful,” the students cheered.

“It is our national bird. If we protect this grove, birds will also be protected,” Anand said.

“Wow, we should come during the monsoon to see them dance,” Sujatha said.

“Sure, we can visit again. You can also see other flowers, fruits, birds, and insects during the monsoon,” I said.

“Yes, different seasons bring with them different things for one to observe. See the barks of the trees are coated with mud at this time of the year. That is termite infestation. During the rainy season, it will wash away,” Prabhakar replied.

“I found this on the way. Look at these fruits. They have a hairy cover like a sweater. What is this plant?” a student showed a stem with a flower and fruit that he had picked up.

“I know this is a passion flower plant,” Anand replied.

“It is called Passiflora foetida. The fruit is partially enclosed by its persistent, deeply divided, and sticky floral bracts. It can trap insects on its bracts. They exude a sticky substance, which contains digestive enzymes. This minimizes predation on young flowers and fruits.Whether or not it gains nourishment from its prey is uncertain. So, it is considered a protocarnivorous plant, which means that it can trap and kill insects, but cannot directly digest or absorb nutrients from its prey like a carnivorous plant. The morphological adaptations, such as sticky trichomes or pitfall traps of protocarnivorous plants resemble the traps of carnivorous plants,” I explained.

“Where can we see carnivorous plants?” a student asked.

I have seen them in the dry forests of Karnataka and the Khasi hills of Meghalaya. You will find them in botanical gardens too. Let me show you a picture. Do you see a resemblance between this Passiflora and Drosera or Sundew?

“Drosera lures, captures, and digests insects using stalked mucilaginous glands that cover the surface of its leaves. The insects are used to supplement the mineral nutrition of the soil in which the plants grow,” Prabhakar explained.

“It's bright and beautiful. I want to see it in its natural habitat. I have seen a pitcher plant in a plant nursery,” a student said.

“Come, let’s go inside the temple now,” Muniyappa led everyone inside the Gangamma Devi temple.

“Will we get prasadam in this temple?” a student asked with a smile.

“Sure. If Ganangamma Devi blesses us, we will get sufficient rain and grow crops, that will serve us throughout the year. Isn’t that good prasadam? So, pray to Gangamma,” Muniyappa said.

All of us proceeded to offer our prayers.

Suggested activities

  1. Make a list of the state birds and trees of India.
  2. Enumerate the types of plant-insect interactions.

References

1. Nandini D, 2023. Divine Plants, RIWATCH, Arunachal Pradesh.
2. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalpavriksha
3. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Drosera

Nandini D is a botanist who has been involved in teaching and research for more than 25 years. Currently, she is engaged as a consultant for Intellectual Property Rights for Karnataka State Council for Science and Technology, Bengaluru, and is a visiting faculty member at RIWATCH, Roing, Arunachal Pradesh. She can be reached at nandinidholepat@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat
The excursion to Tamarind Grove ends with a quiet dialogue between faith and nature, revealing how belief has long guided environmental care. The sacred pipal tree stands as a living keystone – medicinal, ecological, and deeply revered. Through timeless symbols like the Kalpavruksh, ancient traditions have protected biodiversity with wisdom and intent. Encounters with protocarnivorous and carnivorous plants stirred scientific wonder, while the idea of harvest as divine grace fostered humility and gratitude – binding the spiritual and the practical in a profound reverence for the natural world.

item.title: Nurturing number sense: from counting to calculus
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Monica Kochar

HumaneMath is a 25-article series, divided into 5 sections, that transforms mathematics education from rote learning into joyful discovery. Drawing from three decades of classroom experience across diverse Indian educational settings, this series provides teachers with research-backed strategies that honour cultural heritage while embracing innovative pedagogy. Each article combines compelling classroom stories with immediate practical resources, covering every aspect of mathematics education from building foundational mindsets to creating transformative learning experiences. Whether you teach in a rural government school or an urban private institution, whether you work with LKG students discovering numbers or class 12 students mastering calculus, this series offers culturally authentic, educationally sound guidance that respects both our rich mathematical heritage and the diverse needs of contemporary Indian learners. This is more than a teaching resource – this is a roadmap for creating mathematics classrooms where every student can think, grow, and succeed.

Section 1: Building a mathematical mindset

Mathematical success begins not with computational skill but with a mathematical mindset – the fundamental beliefs students develop about mathematics, about their own capabilities, and about how mathematical learning happens. This section explores how to nurture number sense that grows from concrete counting to abstract reasoning, develop mathematical language that spans from playground conversations and academic discourse, foster curiosity that transforms classrooms into communities of mathematical inquiry, build confidence that turns "I can't" into "I'm learning," and create connections that reveal mathematics in every corner of students' lives. These five foundational articles provide the bedrock upon which all effective mathematics education rests, offering strategies that work across all grade levels while honouring the developmental journey from early childhood wonder to adolescent reasoning.

Last week, I was observing a grade 1 classroom, when six-year-old Arjun suddenly jumped up from his seat. He'd been quietly arranging his counting bears, and suddenly he called out, "Ma'am! Ma'am!", practically bouncing with excitement. "If I have 3 groups of 4 bears, that's the same as 4 groups of 3 bears!"

You should have seen his face. Pure joy.

His teacher, Mrs. Sharma, caught my eye and smiled. We both knew we'd witnessed something special – a child discovering the beauty of mathematics on his own. Arjun had just stumbled upon the commutative property of multiplication without anyone teaching him the term or the rule.

This spark, this intuitive understanding that Arjun showed – this is number sense. And here's what I've learned after 30 years in classrooms: it's the foundation upon which all mathematical understanding is built.

What exactly is number sense? (and why should we care?)

As Bobis (1996) describes it, number sense is "a well-organized conceptual framework of number information that enables a person to understand numbers and number relationships and to solve mathematical problems that are not bound by traditional algorithms." Think of it as mathematical common sense – the ability to think flexibly about numbers.

You know how some of your students can look at 47 + 29 and immediately say, "That's close to 76" without calculating? Or instinctively know that if 25 × 4 = 100, then 25 × 8 must be 200? That's number sense in action.

Let me give you a real example. Last month, I was in Mrs. Patel's grade 3 classroom in Mumbai. She posed this problem: "If one samosa costs `8, how much would 4 samosas cost?"

Instead of immediately teaching 4 × 8 = 32, she asked, "How might you figure this out?"

Listen to what happened:

  • Rohan started skip-counting: "8, 16, 24, 32!"
  • Priya used repeated addition: "8 + 8 = 16, then 16 + 8 = 24, then 24 + 8 = 32!"
  • Meera closed her eyes and said, "I'm imagining 4 groups of 8 rupee coins."

Three different paths to the same answer. Three students showing me they understand what multiplication really means, not just how to do it. This is what researchers call "adaptive expertise" – the ability to solve new problems flexibly (Hatano & Inagaki, 1986).

The journey begins: early years (classes 1-5)

Research consistently shows that early number sense is the strongest predictor of later mathematical achievement (Jordan et al., 2009). If we get the foundation right in the primary years, everything else becomes so much easier.

Let me tell you about Mr. Kumar's class 5 classroom in Bengaluru. He was teaching addition of fractions, and instead of jumping straight to the "find the common denominator" rule, he started with a story.

"We're making ladoos for the school festival," he said. "We used 1/4 cup of sugar for one batch and 1/3 cup for another. How much sugar did we use altogether?"

The students dove in with everything they had:

  • Some drew circles and divided them into fourths and thirds.
  • Others used actual measuring cups with coloured water.
  • A few worked on number lines.
  • One group actually measured out flour and sugar.

What happened next was beautiful. The students discovered for themselves that 1/4 + 1/3 couldn't possibly equal 2/7. "That doesn't make sense," said Anjali. "We used more than half a cup, but 2/7 is way less than that!"

These students weren't memorizing a rule about common denominators. They were developing what research calls "number magnitude understanding" – the ability to judge whether an answer makes sense (Siegler & Booth, 2004).

When students have a strong number sense:

  • They check if their answers make sense.
  • They can estimate before calculating.
  • They see patterns and relationships.
  • They're not afraid to try different approaches.

The abstract transition: middle years (classes 6-8)

This is where many students start to struggle. They're moving from concrete arithmetic to abstract concepts like negative numbers and variables. If their number sense foundation is shaky, this is where it shows.

I was recently in Mr. Desai's grade 7 classroom in Pune when he introduced integers. Instead of starting with rules like "negative times negative equals positive," he began with familiar situations.

"Yesterday the temperature was 5°C above normal," he said. "Today, it dropped by 8°C from yesterday's temperature. Where is today's temperature compared to the normal?"

You could see the wheels turning. Students were thinking about elevators going below ground floor, about owing money to friends, about temperatures below freezing point. When they eventually learned that (-3) × (-2) = +6, it wasn't just a rule – they could explain it.

"If the temperature is dropping by 3 degrees each hour," Priya explained, "then 2 hours ago it was 6 degrees warmer than now." She got it. Really got it.

This grounding of abstract concepts in concrete experiences is what makes the difference between procedural knowledge and conceptual understanding.

Mathematical maturity: high school (classes 9-12)

Let me share something that happened in Ms. Agarwal's grade 11 classroom in Delhi. She was introducing exponential functions, and instead of starting with f(x) = ax, she told a story.

"A rumor spreads through our school. On day 1, Sneha tells 3 friends. On day 2, each of those 3 friends tells 3 more friends. This continues every day. How many new people hear the rumor each day?"

The students built a table together, graphed it, and talked about how the growth rate itself was growing. When these students encounter calculus next year, they'll have intuition about exponential functions. They'll understand why these functions grow so dramatically.

This is mathematical maturity – students who can estimate, check their work for reasonableness, and see connections between different representations.

Practical steps for every grade

For primary teachers (classes 1-5): Start every math period with 5 minutes of number sense. Ask questions like:

  • "What's another way to make 15?"
  • "Is 67 closer to 60 or 70? How do you know?"
  • "If you had `50 and spent `23, about how much would you have left?"

Before any calculation, ask for estimates. Make it a game. Celebrate creative thinking, not just right answers.

For middle school teachers (classes 6-8): Connect everything to things your students care about. When teaching percentages, use cricket statistics or mobile phone battery life. When working with negative numbers, talk about elevator floors or temperature changes.

Ask students to explain their thinking. Always. Ask them questions like: "How did you know that?", "Why does that make sense?", "What would happen if...?"

For high school teachers (classes 9-12): Use graphing whenever possible. Let students visualize the calculations, not just manipulate symbols. Connect new concepts to familiar ones. Help them see the big picture of how mathematical ideas connect across topics.

The long view

Arjun's discovery about his counting bears wasn't just a cute moment. It was the beginning of a mathematical journey, which if nurtured right, will lead him to understand calculus with the same wonder and intuition.

Number sense doesn't develop overnight. It grows slowly, through hundreds of meaningful experiences with numbers and patterns. We can't rush it, and we can't replace it with memorized procedures.

When we take the time to build this foundation properly, we give our students mathematical confidence, flexible thinking abilities, and access to the beauty and power of mathematics.

That's why I became a math teacher in the first place.

Quick resources you can use

For younger students (classes 1-3):

  • Daily estimation games: "How many steps from the door to the blackboard?"
  • "Which is more?" activities with small collections of objects.
  • Number stories: "I'm thinking of a number between 10 and 20..."

For middle grades (classes 4-6):

  • "Close enough" calculations: "47 × 3 is close to..."
  • Multiple ways challenges: "Show me three ways to calculate 15 × 12"
  • Real-world connections: Use local prices, distances, temperatures.

For older students (classes 7-12):

  • "Does this make sense?" checks after every calculation.
  • Graphical predictions before algebraic work.
  • "Explain to a younger student" challenges.

Remember: you're not adding more work to your day. You're changing how you approach the work you're already doing.

References

  1. Bobis, J. (1996). Visualisation and the development of number sense with kindergarten children. In J. Mulligan & M. Mitchelmore (Eds.), Children's Number Learning: A Research Monograph (pp. 17-33). Adelaide: AAMT.
  2. Hatano, G., & Inagaki, K. (1986). Two courses of expertise. In H. Stevenson, H. Azuma, & K. Hakuta (Eds.), Child development and education in Japan (pp. 262-272). New York: Freeman.
  3. Jordan, N. C., Glutting, J., & Ramineni, C. (2009). The importance of number sense to mathematics achievement in first and third grades. Learning and Individual Differences, 20(2), 82-88.
  4. National Council of Teachers of Mathematics. (1989). Curriculum and evaluation standards for school mathematics. Reston, VA: Author.
  5. Siegler, R. S., & Booth, J. L. (2004). Development of numerical estimation in young children. Child Development, 75(2), 428-444.

Monica Kochar is a seasoned educator and curriculum designer specializing in math education. With over 10 years of experience, she created HumaneMath, a human-centric math program, and has worked with leading organizations in edtech, publishing, and training. Her expertise spans curriculum design, teacher training, assessments, and quality review. Monica has contributed to key startups, including WhiteHat Jr., and mentored several initiatives. She holds a Master’s in Education from the University of the People. She can be reached at humanemath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monica Kochar
The HumaneMath series reimagines mathematics as a joyful exploration rather than as a mechanical routine, anchoring abstract ideas in lived experience and cultural meaning. At its heart is the cultivation of a strong number sense, empowering students to think flexibly, reason intuitively, and move beyond rigid algorithms. Through engaging, relatable narratives – from counting bears in early grades to tracking temperature shifts in middle school – the series equips educators with practical strategies to nurture confident, curious mathematical thinkers who recognize the beauty and relevance of math in everyday life.

item.title: Diversity, discrimination, and reservations
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prakash Iyer

Ira: Thelast time we talked about debates in the Constituent Assembly on reservation of seats in the state assemblies and the parliament. In this context, I completely understand the need for diverse voices and knowledge. The points made by Nichols Roy about indigenous communities made it even more clear. In the context of cultures, I understand that norms and standards are based on predominant community practices, so alternative voices are needed in order to challenge norms.

But why do we need diverse voices to be represented in the context of jobs or in schools? Norms and standards, with respect to jobs, are and should be fixed, so that expectations are equal and clear irrespective of caste or community. Some groups have been excluded for a long time and so there is a need to make their entry into jobs possible. How is representation relevant here?

Anubhuti: I did say that the idea behind reservations in the employment sector is representation and voices, not merely jobs and incomes. Let’s try and understand what kind of jobs there are and why they cannot be reduced to standard norms which should apply equally to everyone. Let’s think in terms of careers, not merely jobs. Thinking in terms of careers allows us to include learning, growth, and promotions, not just doing a job and getting the salary.

There are the entry level jobs that require minimum capacities – literacy, numeracy, and some reasoning – like clerical jobs. Higher order jobs in the same area require higher order skills like managing people, interpreting norms for both regular functioning and managing exceptional situations, and most importantly managing the work culture and ethos of the workplace. All these skills can be gained only in the workplace, and that completely depends on the nature of interaction between the people working there and the extant culture, which usually manifests in daily practices. It could be simple things like the manner in which people in higher positions are approached, deciding who fills in for an absent colleague, or even chats during coffee break. In theory, it is entirely possible that the work culture is founded on the predominant caste or community’s ways of thinking, or they are favourable to some groups but not others.

This has three serious implications – whether the workplace is truly inclusive or not, whether everybody is getting the opportunity to learn and grow, and whether everyone gets to analyze or question these norms and practices.

Ira: You mean to say that biases or prejudices towards others could manifest in such a way that some people might only be earning money from the job with little possibility of growth? But then they can learn all this through a course or something. That would reduce their dependence on the workplace to learn.

Anubhuti: Ira, we are both teachers. Don’t we know that learning does not happen only through reading and writing? All knowledge requires the experience of practice also. Especially of what we know as soft skills, they come only from experience. No book can educate us in skills and abilities.

More importantly, the ability to analyze and question norms is not just a skill; it requires courage and a deep sense of egalitarianism – that everyone is equal and can participate in rational dialogue without bias or prejudice.

This is when, often unknown to ourselves, biases manifest in behaviours. It could be as subtle as thinking “what the other person is saying sounds logical, but not practical, OR I don’t like this way of working, OR I don’t like his accent or his manner of speaking.” In such situations, it is entirely possible – or is often the case – that deep-set casteist prejudices influence us. In reality, any disagreement or conflict can lead either to patronizing behaviour, condescension, or verbal violence.

Ira: Thisreminds me of Vijay Tendulkar’s play Kanyadaan, which I read recently.The Dalit activist resents the social reformer because he perceives that they are claiming to reform him and his community into being more human. His retaliation is through extremely vulgar and violent language – as if to show the reformer “This is how you think of me.”

So, are you saying that the purpose of reservation is to some extent also reverse reformation? Reforming the system and culture so that it includes not only hitherto excluded people, but also their norms and ways of thinking.

Anubhuti: That is very well put. You see this is why I said multiculturalists say their voices should be included, and the system should not aim for integration or reformation. If there is any reformation, it should be of the existing work culture and structures immanent in society, not the people who have been marginalized or excluded so far.

Ira: That is why you quoted Madhav Khosla as saying the Constitution is meant to be a pedagogical tool to educate the Indian society into democracy, and egalitarianism is the foundation of a democratic society. And true inclusion will have happened only if people from different castes or communities can engage in a rational dialogue on existing norms and practices.

How do you bring all this into the classroom? It is easy for us adults to theorize and expound on this, but how do you communicate this to young children? Surely, they are not as deeply prejudiced as us adults are wont to be.

Anubhuti: There are two aspects to it – an adaptable curriculum and creating space for dialogue in the classroom. When we teach, we build a bridge between what children already know and the new knowledge they are expected to learn. Sometimes we need to adjust the curriculum to include all groups, especially the civics curriculum, in which there is only a passing reference to oppressed groups as Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes.

Remember, a while back I was sharing how I brought in caste discrimination in class and one student claimed that it did not happen in his house and used the phrase upper caste which angered another student?[i] You pointed out how I was wrong in the way I dealt with the situation.

Ira: Yes, you introduced a discussion on the existence of caste discrimination in class, but you stopped it because emotions ran high and you said your priority was to calm the atmosphere.

Anubhuti: I have learnt a lot from that conversation with you and Amrita, our history teacher. I thought about the boy (A) who claimed untouchability was not practiced by educated people like his parents, and the angry reaction of the Dalit student (B). The issue was that A had formed an understanding of the identity of Dalits based on his parents’ and family’s concepts and views, which did seem to contain a patronizing gaze. That made B angry because he wanted to be perceived as an equal, not someone who needs patronage.

The effect was a result of the difference in their gazes – the so-called upper-caste gaze was being confronted by the so-called lower-caste gaze demanding formal equality. A could not see that at all, which is why he was astonished by the anger.

Ira: Yes, and we needed to deal with those emotions in a way that would help them engage in a rational dialogue. That is a tough job. But this is a pedagogical method; why do you say adaptable curriculum?

Anubhuti: You see, the civics curriculum has the Constitution and an explanation of reservation and its need. If you share this in these abstract terms – which the curriculum expects – you will be essentializing groups of people or just reiterating the existence of caste discrimination in India. We need to connect this to daily experiences, for which we will have to make additions to the curriculum during its implementation in classrooms. Only then can we be convinced that the curricular objectives are met.

Ira: How will you do it when you are teaching the Constitution and reservation? You have explained the logic of the Constitution in great detail to me, and yet you are saying that is not enough and we have to do more.

Anubhuti: Yes, like I said, my analysis of the two boys, A and B, was that their individual perspectives or gazes were a result of their life experiences – for A it was experiences as a privileged family who could choose to discriminate or not, and for B it was life experiences as a Dalit when he had to either face discrimination silently or rebel in anger.

The felt emotion is shame. Dalits are expected not to feel shame if they are treated with condescension or patronage. Imagine how it would make B feel if he is expected to be thankful for the additional effort A is putting to be patronizing?

I think it is important for A and the rest of the class to at least imagine what shame of this kind felt like, for which we would have to invoke memories of shame they went through. Then we could ask B what he thought of how A felt when he was put to shame and do the same with B sharing his experience and asking A how he would feel if he was treated like that on a daily basis.

This cannot happen only through verbal dialogue. I think doing something like a role-play or asking them to enact a play would work better since they would have to put in the effort to evoke the emotion experienced by the character. I am considering screening Satgati, a short film by Satyajit Ray based on a short story written by Premchand. A brahmin man asks a starving Dalit to cut a huge log of wood in his house. He doesn’t even let him enter his house or give him water to drink. The Dalit also thinks he will be committing a sin if he steps inside the house. He eventually dies of hunger and fatigue while cutting the tree.[ii]

I will screen this and ask the class how they would feel IF they were the Dalit character in the film, and what is wrong in this situation and who is responsible.

Ira: That would be helpful. It is the closest we can come to helping everyone go through others’ life experiences. At least, there is the possibility of some empathy being generated.

And you will also avoid essentialization of groups of people as oppressors or oppressed. But isn’t that historizing caste? Premchand’s stories were all in the 19th century. Some students might think this was how it was in the past, but not now.

Anubhuti: That is true. The earlier NCERT “Social and Political Life” textbooks had an excerpt from Omprakash Valmiki’s autobiography Jhootan.[iii] I could use that.

Basically, I am thinking of using literature, cinema, or some art form, to teach about this since it is bound to invoke strong emotions. Externalizing a real situation into fiction helps one think clearly; and good art invokes our true feelings and responses – whether we express it to others or not.

[i] https://teacherplus.org/2023/2023/february-2023/pedagogical-neutrality-and-conflicts-in-the-classroom/

[ii] Hindi short film Satgati – https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rRpaPV3QayY

[iii] https://www.anujjindal.in/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/Indian-Society-7th-Class.pdf

Prakash Iyer teaches Philosophy of Education and Curriculum theory at Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. He can be reached at prakash.iyer@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prakash Iyer
Ira and Anubhuti’s continued dialogue reframes diversity and reservations not as concessions, but as vital correctives to systems shaped by dominant norms. It exposes how careers are quietly derailed by exclusionary cultures and everyday prejudices, arguing that the problem lies not with marginalized individuals but with the structures themselves – calling for a “reverse reformation” of institutions. In education, Anubhuti advocates a living curriculum that connects constitutional ideals to students’ real encounters with shame, patronage, and bias. Through literature, cinema, and art, classrooms can nurture empathy, self-reflection, and a deeper understanding of formal equality.

item.title: Unifying language and art: a lesson plan on Ashoka
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Aastha Arya

In modern education, fostering interdisciplinary connections between subjects is indispensable to nurturing well-rounded individuals. This article presents an innovative lesson plan that integrates language with artistic expression to explore peace through the lens of Ashoka, the Indian emperor known as “the messenger of peace”.

At the heart of this lesson plan lies an exploration of Ashoka's transformation following the Kalinga war, which led him to embrace the principles of non-violence and compassion. When done in my classroom, the lesson commenced with an ice-breaking activity, which encouraged students to share their knowledge about preachers of peace whether from history or the present times. This initial engagement set the stage for deeper exploration and discourse.

Incorporating the essence of Buddhism, students engaged in a meditation session focussed on cultivating inner peace and mindfulness. Drawing inspiration from Ashoka's embrace of Buddhist teachings, this session allowed students to experience the transformative power of meditation in fostering personal wellbeing and promoting peace within themselves and their surroundings. Students were then taken on a virtual expedition to delve into Ashoka's edicts via Google Earth, prompting critical analysis and reflection on the enduring messages of peace and tolerance. Through this immersive experience, students gained insight into Ashoka's commitment to fostering harmony within his empire and beyond.

The lesson started with a graphic organizer, where a mind map on the theme of peace was created on the blackboard with inputs from the students. This was followed by an interesting activity, where they drew and then spoke about a symbol that represented peace for them and spoke about its symbolic reference.

After this, the students read an allotted portion of the lesson within a given time limit. They then summarized what they had read and formulated two questions from their section. The other students, with the aid of the AMP box*, provided answers to those questions. All students then analyzed Ashoka's transformation post-Kalinga War, drawing connections between his principles of governance and contemporary issues of social justice. Students engaged in vocabulary exercises related to the lesson, expanding their language skills while reinforcing the theme of the lesson. This was followed by the creation of a character web chart of Ashoka, wherein students shared various characteristics of Ashoka before and after his transformation, exploring his motivations, virtues, and impact on history. The concept of ‘metamorphosis’ was discussed in relation to Ashoka; just as a caterpillar transforms into a butterfly, so did Ashoka after the Kalinga war.

Students worked in groups of four and made a list of keywords as well as vocabulary words from the lesson. They found the meaning of new and difficult words using dictionaries. The group that found the meaning first received bonus points. Students wrote the meaning, as well as the part of speech, of the words in their English notebooks. The discussion then narrowed down to Ashoka, his emphasis on religious tolerance, and his peaceful efforts to establish Buddhism as a major world religion. This further led to a discussion on the importance and relevance of peace in the present times – how men of religion today have seldom been as tolerant as Ashoka, and how he became a global ambassador of peace with his thoughtful and profound actions. Students worked in pairs and framed key points for their answers in class. They attempted practice worksheet questions and wrote analytical answers in their English notebooks.

The topic was enriched with integration with social science, wherein students identified and marked the places where Ashokan edicts are present in India. They used the highlight feature on the smart board to mark the edicts on a map of India. By marking significant landmarks on the map, students explored India’s connection to Ashoka's legacy.

Thereafter, the students embarked on a creative journey where they composed a song on the theme of peace using relevant words, employing critical thinking, and innovative approaches to lyrical and melodic creation. They were encouraged to play with different objects and create rhythms or beats. To enhance their musical performance, some students utilized technology to amalgamate their videos. This integration of technology with the activity empowered students to develop media literacy skills while showcasing their artistic talents in a digital format. The lesson culminated with an extended activity, where the students wrote an article expressing their views on the theme of peace.

Conclusion

The integration of art, as well as other subjects in this lesson, provided students with a comprehensive understanding of peace while fostering creativity, critical thinking, and collaboration. By exploring Ashoka's legacy through diverse artistic mediums, students gained insight into the universal values of empathy and compassion, empowering them to become catalysts for positive change in their communities.

This lesson plan served as a model for integrating art in interdisciplinary education, offering educators innovative strategies to engage students in meaningful exploration of complex themes such as peace and social justice.
